statement: Obsidian is a local-first, Markdown-based knowledge management app. Its plugin API wraps CodeMirror 6 (editor), Electron (desktop runtime), and a custom Vault abstraction (file system). Plugins run in a Node.js-like environment with filesystem access (desktop only). Core philosophy: Obsidian owns the Vault (folder of .md files), plugins are guests. UNSAID: mobile plugins have a restricted API — no Node.js built-ins, no native modules. Desktop plugins that use require('fs') directly will fail on mobile.

Logseq is an outliner-based PKM tool with a graph database (Datascript) under the hood. Unlike Obsidian (tree of markdown files), Logseq's data model is a flat graph of blocks with parent/child relationships. Plugins use the logseq.Editor API and Datascript queries for data access. UNSAID: Logseq is transitioning to a new DB version (Logseq DB) that breaks backward compatibility with existing plugins. Plugins for classic Logseq may not work with DB version.

Obsidian plugins extend Plugin class with onload() and onunload(). Resources registered in onload() MUST be released in onunload() to prevent memory leaks. this.registerEvent(), this.addCommand(), this.addRibbonIcon() auto-unregister on unload. UNSAID: raw addEventListener() calls on DOM elements or app.workspace.on() WITHOUT using this.registerEvent() will leak — the event handler persists after the plugin is disabled/reloaded. This is the most common source of ghost behavior after plugin updates.

Obsidian's Vault API (this.app.vault) is the ONLY correct way to read/write files. Direct Node.js fs operations bypass Obsidian's cache and event system — changes won't trigger vault.on('modify') events and the metadata cache won't update. Key methods: vault.read(), vault.create(), vault.modify(), vault.rename(), vault.delete(). For metadata (frontmatter, links), use MetadataCache: app.metadataCache.getFileCache(). Extraction signal: plugins using require('fs') for vault files are doing it wrong.

Obsidian plugin settings use loadData()/saveData() for persistence (stored in .obsidian/plugins/<id>/data.json). The PluginSettingTab class provides the settings UI panel. UNSAID: settings are stored as plain JSON — no schema migration built in. When plugin updates add new settings keys, old users' data.json won't have them. Best practice: use Object.assign({}, DEFAULT_SETTINGS, await this.loadData()) to merge defaults with saved settings, ensuring new keys get their defaults.

Obsidian's editor is CodeMirror 6. Custom decorations, syntax highlighting, and inline widgets use CodeMirror 6's extension system (ViewPlugin, StateField, Decoration). UNSAID: CodeMirror 6 is a reactive system — extensions react to state transactions. Performing DOM manipulation directly on editor elements (not via CM6 decorations) will be wiped on the next CM6 render cycle. Extraction signal: plugins that modify .cm-editor DOM directly are fragile; correct plugins use ViewPlugin with decorations.

Obsidian Dataview plugin enables SQL-like queries over vault metadata. Basic TABLE query: `TABLE file.mtime, status FROM #project WHERE status != "done" SORT file.mtime DESC`. Inline queries: `= this.status` renders the current file's frontmatter value inline. UNSAID: Dataview reads frontmatter (YAML between `---`) and inline fields (`key:: value` syntax). Dataview is read-only — it cannot modify files. Performance degrades with large vaults (>5000 notes) because Dataview indexes the entire vault in memory. The `dataviewjs` query type gives full JavaScript access for complex aggregations but requires understanding Dataview's async API (`dv.pages()`, `dv.table()`). Dataview queries are live — they re-render on vault changes, which causes visible flickering in large dashboards.

Obsidian sync conflict resolution: when the same note is edited on two devices before sync, Obsidian Sync creates a conflict copy (`Note (conflict 1).md`). UNSAID: conflict copies are silent — there's no notification in the UI, only a new file in the vault. Without a periodic conflict review process, conflicted notes accumulate unresolved. The Obsidian Sync conflict strategy is last-write-wins at the paragraph level (not file level) — but this only works when both devices are online and syncing regularly. Offline editing on mobile + desktop creates the highest conflict rate. Workaround: use a Dataview query to surface all `(conflict)` files: `LIST FROM "" WHERE contains(file.name, "conflict")`. iCloud sync + Obsidian (without Obsidian Sync) is a common source of data corruption — iCloud's sync timing and .obsidian/ directory conflicts with plugin state files.

Obsidian graph view visualizes wikilink connections between notes. The local graph (per-note) shows connections up to a specified depth. Useful for: discovering orphan notes, finding unexpected connections, validating MOC (Map of Content) structure. UNSAID: the global graph view is visually impressive but cognitively overwhelming for vaults >200 notes — most users find the local graph more actionable. Graph analysis insight: high-degree nodes (many incoming links) identify hub concepts; isolated nodes indicate notes that need linking or archival. Obsidian's graph doesn't show Dataview virtual links — only explicit `[[wikilinks]]`. For programmatic graph analysis (clustering, centrality), export links via Dataview and process with Python/networkx outside Obsidian.

Obsidian Templater plugin (community) extends the built-in Templates core plugin with dynamic templates. Templater enables: `<% tp.date.now('YYYY-MM-DD') %>` for dates, `<% tp.file.title %>` for current file name, `<% await tp.system.prompt('Enter value') %>` for user input. Templates can run JavaScript functions. UNSAID: Templater's `tp.file.rename()` and `tp.file.move()` enable auto-organization — templates that create a file in a temp location then move it based on metadata. The `on_new_file_creation` trigger runs Templater automatically when a new file is created in a configured folder. Common mistake: using Templater syntax in the built-in Templates plugin (or vice versa) — the syntax is different (`{{title}}` vs `<% tp.file.title %>`).

Obsidian CSS snippets enable vault-wide visual customization. Snippets are CSS files placed in `.obsidian/snippets/` and enabled per-snippet in Settings → Appearance. CSS variables (--color-base-00 through --color-base-100, --font-text-size, etc.) are the correct override targets — theme-compatible and don't break on theme updates. UNSAID: direct element CSS targeting (`div.cm-line { }`) breaks when Obsidian updates CodeMirror internals — CSS variables are stable contracts. The Style Settings community plugin provides a UI for theme CSS variables without writing CSS. A common use case: callout styling — `[data-callout='warning'] { --callout-color: 220, 50, 50; }` customizes specific callout types. Mobile and desktop render the same CSS snippets — use `@media (max-width: 768px)` for device-specific overrides.

Logseq Datalog/Datascript queries give programmatic access to the graph database. Basic query: `{{query (and [[ProjectA]] (task TODO))}}` finds blocks tagged with ProjectA that have TODO status. Advanced queries use EDN format: `{:title "My Query" :query [:find (pull ?b [*]) :where [?b :block/marker "TODO"] [?b :block/refs [:block/name "projecta"]]]}`. UNSAID: Logseq queries operate on BLOCKS not pages — the unit is a bullet point, not a file. This is fundamentally different from Obsidian Dataview (which operates on files). Logseq's new DB version (2024+) changes the underlying schema — EDN queries written for the classic file-based version will need rewriting for DB version. The `:block/content` field contains the raw block text including Markdown syntax.

Community plugin selection criteria for Obsidian: prefer plugins with (1) >10k downloads, (2) active GitHub commits within 6 months, (3) compatible with current Obsidian API version (`minAppVersion` in manifest.json). The Obsidian plugin API changes across major versions — plugins using deprecated APIs show yellow warnings in Settings. UNSAID: installing many plugins has real performance costs — each plugin's `onload()` runs at startup, and active plugins consume memory. The BRAT (Beta Reviewer's Auto-update Tester) community plugin enables installing pre-release plugins from GitHub — useful for testing but risky for production vaults. Core plugins (bundled with Obsidian) are maintained by the Obsidian team and receive compatibility updates with each release — prefer core plugins over community alternatives when functionality overlaps.

Obsidian vs Logseq trade-off: Obsidian is file-first (Markdown files are primary, app is a viewer), Logseq is graph-first (database is primary, files are export). Obsidian's advantage: portability (open any .md file in any editor), ecosystem compatibility (git, Pandoc, static site generators). Logseq's advantage: outliner UX (daily notes + block references + bidirectional links are first-class), built-in task management, journaling workflow. UNSAID: Logseq's new DB version (2024+) abandons the file-first model entirely — data lives in a proprietary database, not Markdown files. This is a fundamental philosophical shift that makes Logseq DB less portable than classic Logseq. For teams requiring version-controlled, git-diffable notes, Obsidian remains the clear choice. For personal daily journaling with outliner workflow, Logseq (classic) remains superior.

Obsidian backup best practices: the `.obsidian/` folder contains all plugin data, settings, and workspace state — it must be included in backups. Git-based backup strategy: commit the entire vault including `.obsidian/` for full reproducibility. Use `.gitignore` to exclude: `.obsidian/workspace.json` (per-session state that changes on every open), `.obsidian/cache` (rebuild-able), large attachments. UNSAID: Obsidian Sync and Git sync running simultaneously on the same vault cause race conditions — Obsidian Sync changes file timestamps which triggers git dirty state. Use one or the other, not both. The Obsidian Git community plugin provides automatic commit+push on vault changes — configure `Vault backup interval` to 20+ minutes to avoid commit spam. For mobile backup without Obsidian Sync, iCloud (iOS) or Dropsync (Android) work but require careful .obsidian/ exclusion to avoid cross-platform plugin config conflicts.

Logseq plugin development pitfalls: Logseq's plugin SDK (`@logseq/libs`) wraps a postMessage-based IFrame communication channel — all plugin API calls are async even when they look synchronous. UNSAID: `logseq.Editor.insertBlock()` returns a Promise — missing the await causes race conditions where subsequent operations run before the block exists. The `logseq.DB.onChanged()` callback fires for EVERY graph change — expensive computations in this callback block the main thread and degrade editor responsiveness. Batch DB operations using `logseq.DB.datascriptQuery()` instead of iterating with `logseq.Editor.getBlock()` per item. Logseq plugin hot-reload requires `logseq.useSettingsSchema()` to be called in `main()` before any other API — plugins that call API methods before `logseq.ready()` resolves will fail silently on some Logseq versions.

Obsidian Canvas (core plugin) enables spatial note-taking — a freeform infinite canvas with note cards, images, and links. Canvas files use `.canvas` JSON format (listable in Vault API). UNSAID: Canvas is not indexed by search or Dataview — links FROM canvas to notes are tracked in the backlinks panel of the target note, but content ON canvas is not searchable. The Obsidian Canvas format is open (JSON), enabling programmatic canvas generation — useful for auto-generating architecture diagrams or concept maps from structured data. Canvas performance degrades with >200 nodes — use nested canvases (embed a canvas inside a canvas) for large diagrams. Cards on a canvas can embed notes as live previews, meaning edits to the underlying note immediately reflect on the canvas without manual refresh.

Obsidian publish workflow: Obsidian Publish is the official hosted option — selective publishing of notes with a custom domain. For self-hosted alternatives: Quartz (static site generator for Obsidian vaults) and Digital Garden plugin (deploys to Netlify/Vercel). Quartz converts wikilinks and Dataview queries to HTML. UNSAID: Obsidian Publish doesn't execute Dataview queries — they render as raw code blocks. Plugins that generate content (Dataview, Templater output) must be replaced with static content before publishing. The `publish: true` frontmatter flag in Obsidian Publish selectively includes notes — without this flag (or with `publish: false`), notes are private. A common mistake: publishing a note that embeds a private note via wikilink — Obsidian Publish breaks the link silently rather than publishing the private embedded note.

Obsidian Dataview plugin enables SQL-like queries over vault metadata, turning the vault into a queryable personal database. UNSAID: Dataview reads YAML frontmatter, inline fields (using '::' syntax), and file metadata (ctime, mtime, size) to generate dynamic tables and lists. The key performance insight: DataviewJS (JavaScript-based queries) is significantly slower than DQL (Dataview Query Language) for large vaults because it bypasses Dataview's index cache and iterates raw file metadata. For vaults with 2000+ notes, DQL queries run in milliseconds while equivalent DataviewJS queries take 200-500ms. Inline Dataview queries (backtick-wrapped) re-evaluate on every render — use sparingly on frequently-visited notes. The 'file.lists' and 'file.tasks' Dataview implicit fields allow querying TODO items across the entire vault, enabling a 'global task board' dashboard that aggregates all outstanding tasks from all notes.

Obsidian vault structure using PARA method (Projects, Areas, Resources, Archive) provides a proven organizational framework that scales from 100 to 10,000+ notes. UNSAID: PARA in Obsidian maps to top-level folders: /Projects (active, time-bound outcomes), /Areas (ongoing responsibilities with no deadline), /Resources (reference material by topic), /Archive (inactive items from other categories). The critical implementation insight: notes should flow through PARA lifecycle — a Resource becomes a Project note when you decide to act on it, then moves to Archive when complete. Tags provide cross-cutting concerns that span PARA boundaries (e.g., '#client/acme' appears in both Projects and Areas). Resist creating deep folder hierarchies — 3 levels maximum. The Obsidian Folder Notes plugin (community) allows a folder to also be a note, enabling folder overview notes. MOCs (Maps of Content) in a /MOC folder supplement PARA by providing thematic entry points across all four categories.

evidence_refs.description: PARA method by Tiago Forte for knowledge organization

statement: Obsidian sync conflict resolution fails silently when multiple devices edit the same note simultaneously without Obsidian Sync, creating duplicate conflicted copies with arbitrary name suffixes. UNSAID: Obsidian Sync (paid) handles real-time conflict resolution with version history. Using third-party sync (iCloud, Dropbox, Google Drive) with Obsidian creates race conditions: if two devices save a note within the same sync window, the sync service creates a conflicted copy (e.g., 'note (John's MacBook's conflicted copy 2024-03-15).md') that Obsidian treats as a completely new unlinked note. Backlinks to the original note do NOT transfer to the conflicted copy. Detection: a Dataview query can find all notes matching '*(conflicted copy)*' pattern. Resolution: must manually compare and merge conflicted copies using a diff tool. Prevention: use Obsidian Sync, or git-based sync (obsidian-git plugin with auto-commit on change), which merges conflicts with standard git semantics rather than file duplication.

Obsidian Templater plugin enables dynamic note creation with JavaScript-powered templates, far surpassing the built-in Templates core plugin's static text substitution. UNSAID: Templater uses '<%' tags for static insertion and '<%*' tags for JavaScript execution blocks. The critical difference: Templater templates run when a new note is created from them (via hotkey or file creation trigger), while the built-in Templates plugin only inserts static text. Key Templater patterns: tp.date.now('YYYY-MM-DD') for date stamps, tp.file.rename() to auto-name notes based on content (e.g., a meeting note template that names itself from the first heading), tp.system.prompt() for interactive input during note creation, and tp.web.daily_quote() for external API calls. For PKM workflows, Templater's folder-based auto-template feature automatically applies a template to new notes based on which folder they're created in — essential for PARA where each folder type has different frontmatter requirements.

Logseq's outliner-first philosophy treats every bullet point as a first-class block with its own unique ID, enabling block-level references that Obsidian's page-level linking model cannot replicate. UNSAID: In Logseq, ((block-uuid)) references create embedded block citations that update bidirectionally when the source changes — fundamentally different from Obsidian's [[note]] links that reference entire pages. This block reference model is ideal for Zettelkasten users who want atomic note fragments to appear in multiple contexts. The trade-off: Logseq's block ID system creates files with embedded UUIDs in block properties ('id:: 64abc123') that pollute the markdown with Logseq-specific metadata, making the files less portable. Obsidian users who switch to Logseq often find their existing notes render fine but can't benefit from block references retroactively without manual ID assignment. Block embeds in Logseq are read-only — you must navigate to the source block to edit.

Obsidian as a writing environment for long-form content uses the Longform plugin, combined with Pandoc for export to Word/PDF, creating a professional writing workflow without leaving markdown. UNSAID: Longform plugin organizes a writing project as a collection of scene/chapter notes that can be compiled into a single document in customizable order. The Pandoc integration (community plugin) converts Obsidian-flavored markdown to DOCX, PDF, HTML with citation support. For academic writing, the Citations plugin (Zotero + BibTeX) enables @citation references that Pandoc converts to proper bibliography entries. The workflow: write in Obsidian with [[wiki links]] for cross-references, use Dataview to track word counts across scenes, compile with Longform, export with Pandoc. The key limitation: Obsidian's preview mode doesn't render exactly as the exported output — always export to review final formatting. For collaborative editing, export to DOCX for reviewers, then incorporate feedback back into Obsidian manually.

evidence_refs.description: Longform plugin for long-form writing in Obsidian

statement: Obsidian's wikilink resolution uses a flat namespace for note names — two notes with the same name in different folders create ambiguous links that resolve to the oldest or first-alphabetically matching note. UNSAID: Obsidian resolves ambiguous [[note name]] links by picking the first match in alphabetical folder order, not the most recently created or most linked note. This creates subtle bugs in large vaults where two notes named 'Meeting Notes' in different project folders result in all [[Meeting Notes]] links pointing to only one of them. The fix: use unique note names vault-wide (e.g., 'ACME Meeting Notes' vs 'BigCorp Meeting Notes') or use explicit path-based links ([[Projects/ACME/Meeting Notes]]). The Obsidian community recommends the 'unique note creator' approach (Random Note ID plugin or UID-based naming) for Zettelkasten users who want content-addressed rather than name-addressed notes. Wikilinks can include an alias: [[long note name|short display]] to maintain readability with unique underlying names.

evidence_refs.description: Internal links and wikilink resolution in Obsidian

statement: Logseq queries use Datalog syntax (via org.clj/datascript) for advanced block-level queries that can traverse property relationships, unlike Obsidian Dataview's SQL-like DQL. UNSAID: Logseq's advanced query syntax uses the {:find [...] :where [...]} pattern with Datalog rules. The built-in simple query syntax ([[{{query ...}}]]) covers most use cases (filter by property, tag, date range) without Datalog knowledge. Advanced queries enable powerful patterns: find all blocks that reference a given page within another specific page (nested relationship queries), find blocks with specific property values meeting multiple criteria simultaneously. The key architectural limitation: Logseq queries can only access data stored in the graph database — attachments, images, and PDF annotations are not queryable. Performance degrades significantly for complex queries with multiple join conditions on large graphs (10,000+ blocks). Index-only properties (configured via Logseq settings) are queryable much faster than scanned block properties.

Obsidian community plugin updates can break vault functionality without warning because plugins have no compatibility contract with the Obsidian API versioning system. UNSAID: When Obsidian updates its core API, plugins that use deprecated or changed APIs may silently fail, display errors, or corrupt note formatting. The risk is highest immediately after Obsidian core updates, before plugin authors update their plugins. Mitigation: enable 'Restricted mode' before updating Obsidian (disables all plugins) to identify which plugin causes breakage after re-enabling them one by one. For production writing workflows, delay Obsidian updates by 1-2 weeks until plugin compatibility is confirmed via the community forum. BRAT plugin (Beta Reviewers Auto-update Tool) allows testing pre-release plugin versions but increases breakage risk. The most reliable core plugins (Dataview, Templater, Obsidian Git) have dedicated maintainers with fast patch cycles; niche plugins may go months between updates.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian plugin security and update management

statement: Obsidian-git plugin enables automatic version control for a vault using git, providing backup, history, and multi-device sync as a free alternative to Obsidian Sync. UNSAID: Configure obsidian-git with auto-commit interval (every 5 minutes while Obsidian is open) and auto-pull on Obsidian startup. The critical setup: the git repository should be hosted on a private GitHub/GitLab repo — never a public repo, as vault contents may include sensitive information. Mobile sync is trickier: Obsidian for iOS/Android supports obsidian-git but requires a Working Copy (iOS) or Termux (Android) setup for git authentication. The key limitation: git is file-level, not real-time — edits made on two devices within the same auto-commit window create true git merge conflicts. These conflicts appear as standard conflict markers in the markdown file, which Obsidian renders as raw text. Best practice: always pull before starting a writing session when using multiple devices. Large binary attachments (PDFs, images) should use Git LFS or be stored in a separate folder excluded from git.

Zettelkasten implementation in Obsidian uses atomic note IDs, direct conceptual links (not hierarchical), and the three-note-type system (fleeting, literature, permanent) to build a thinking network rather than an information archive. UNSAID: The key differentiator of a true Zettelkasten from a note archive: permanent notes are written IN YOUR OWN WORDS as complete, self-contained ideas that don't require reading surrounding context to understand. Each permanent note has a unique ID (timestamp-based: YYYYMMDDHHMMSS or sequential: 202401151430) in the filename. Links between permanent notes represent genuine conceptual connections you've identified, not just topic similarity. The Luhmann-style branching ID system (1a, 1a1, 1a1a) maps poorly to folder-based tools — in Obsidian, use sequential IDs + tags for thematic grouping instead. Graph view becomes meaningful only when notes are linked by conceptual relationships — avoid creating links just because two notes mention the same topic. The Zettlr app is purpose-built for academic Zettelkasten and handles citation workflows better than Obsidian.

Obsidian Tasks plugin provides a complete task management system inside the vault, with due dates, recurrence, priorities, and cross-vault filtering via query blocks. UNSAID: Tasks uses special emoji syntax for metadata: 📅 2024-03-15 for due date, 🔁 every week for recurrence, ⏫/🔼/🔽 for priority levels. A tasks query block (```tasks ... ```) renders as a dynamic filtered task list based on any combination of properties. The plugin integrates with Obsidian calendar plugins to show tasks on a calendar view. Critical limitation: Tasks only works within Obsidian — exported markdown shows raw emoji syntax and rendered task blocks become empty in other editors. For teams or external sharing, prefer Todoist/Linear/GitHub Issues integration instead. The 'globally filter' setting allows tasks to only be tracked in notes with specific tags, preventing every checkbox in the vault from becoming a tracked task. Dataview can also query tasks but Tasks plugin provides better UI/UX for task interaction (complete from query result).

Logseq journal-first workflow uses daily pages as the primary capture surface with page references to build a searchable second brain over time. UNSAID: Logseq automatically creates a daily page for each date when you open it (or navigate to today). Rapid capture in daily pages with [[page references]] and #tags automatically builds the graph — you don't need to pre-organize. The emergent organization pattern: topics appear as linked references on their page automatically as you capture daily thoughts. The critical workflow step many skip: end-of-day review to promote important blocks from the daily page to permanent topic pages using block references or copy/paste with source attribution. Without this promotion step, insights get buried in chronological logs. The linked references section at the bottom of every page shows all daily journal entries mentioning that page — making every topic page a curated view of your evolving thinking about it.

Obsidian's mobile app (iOS/Android) has significant feature limitations compared to the desktop app that affect power users relying on plugin-heavy workflows. UNSAID: Many community plugins don't support mobile due to Node.js dependencies, file system access differences, or performance constraints. Dataview works on mobile with some query type limitations. Templater's web fetching functions don't work on iOS due to CORS restrictions. Obsidian-git has complex setup requirements on mobile (requires Working Copy on iOS for git credentials). The mobile app uses the same vault as desktop if synced, but plugins must be individually confirmed working on mobile. The mobile app's keyboard shortcuts differ from desktop — heavy hotkey users need to reconfigure. For capture-focused mobile use, the Obsidian iOS widget and share sheet integration allow quick note creation without opening the full app. Performance: vaults with 5000+ notes experience noticeable lag on older mobile hardware during search and graph view rendering.

Building a research pipeline in Obsidian using Zotero + ZoteroConnector + Dataview + Citations creates an academic knowledge management system that connects reading annotations to original writing. UNSAID: Workflow: 1) Collect papers in Zotero with browser connector, 2) Annotate PDFs in Zotero, 3) Export annotations to Obsidian via Zotero Integration plugin (community) which creates literature notes from Zotero items, 4) Write permanent notes linking back to literature notes via @citation-key syntax, 5) Pandoc export with pandoc-citeproc generates proper bibliography. The ZoteroIntegration plugin template determines how literature notes are formatted — customize to include abstract, tags, and annotation excerpts. Critical: use Zotero's Better BibTeX plugin to generate stable, readable citation keys (AuthorYYYYword format) rather than Zotero's default random keys. The resulting vault becomes a personal research database where Dataview queries can surface all notes citing a specific paper or all literature in a research domain.

Logseq database version (DB Logseq) is a breaking architecture change from the file-based Logseq that makes existing markdown files incompatible with the new storage model. UNSAID: Logseq's DB version (announced 2023, rolling out 2024-2025) stores blocks in a local SQLite database rather than individual markdown files, enabling faster queries and proper block-level sync. However, DB Logseq files are not interchangeable with file-based Logseq — migration requires an official migration tool that converts markdown to DB format, and the reverse migration is lossy (block IDs and properties become comments). Users who rely on Logseq's file-based markdown for interoperability with other tools (Obsidian, vim, git diff of individual notes) should carefully evaluate whether DB Logseq meets their portability requirements before migrating. The DB version introduces proper multi-graph references and block sync across graphs, features that the file-based version cannot support.

evidence_refs.description: Logseq DB version announcement and features

statement: Obsidian graph view becomes a diagnostic tool for knowledge structure quality when interpreted correctly — isolated notes indicate poor linking, dense clusters indicate topic coherence, and bridges indicate interdisciplinary connections. UNSAID: The graph view is not meant for daily navigation (it's too slow and cluttered for large vaults) but for periodic structural review. Filter the graph by tag to see topic-specific connectivity. High-value notes appear as hubs with many connections — if your most important ideas have few links, they're under-referenced. The 'local graph' view (right-click a note) shows only 1-3 hops of connections, which IS useful for daily navigation to discover related notes. Graph visualization in Obsidian uses a force-directed layout that changes every time — don't attempt to memorize position-based spatial memory. Groups (color-coded by folder or tag) make large graphs scannable. For large vaults, use the 'depth' filter to show only notes you've recently modified, giving a working-memory-sized graph.

Obsidian Sync end-to-end encryption means Obsidian cannot recover your vault if you lose the encryption password — there is no account-based recovery mechanism. UNSAID: Obsidian Sync encrypts vaults client-side before upload. The encryption key is derived from your password; Obsidian's servers cannot decrypt your vault. If you forget the password and have no local copy, the vault is permanently unrecoverable. This is by design (privacy-first) but surprises users expecting cloud service recovery options. Mitigation: maintain at least one local backup independent of Obsidian Sync (obsidian-git, Time Machine, manual backup). For vault sharing between users (e.g., couple sharing a household vault), all users must know the sync password. Obsidian Sync's version history (retains deleted/modified files for 12 months) requires correct password to access. The sync password is separate from the Obsidian account login — a common source of confusion when setting up a new device.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian Sync security and encryption documentation

statement: Combining Obsidian with Readwise Reader creates an active reading pipeline where highlights from books, articles, and PDFs automatically flow into Obsidian as literature notes for processing. UNSAID: Readwise Reader (read-later app with highlighting) integrates with Obsidian via the official Readwise plugin that syncs highlights to Obsidian as structured markdown files on a schedule. Configure the sync template to include source URL, author, tags, and the exact highlight text. The processing workflow: Readwise auto-populates a /Readwise/ folder; your job is to process these into your own words in permanent notes. Critical: don't mistake highlight collection for knowledge retention — highlights are input, not processed knowledge. The Daily Review feature in Readwise surfaces old highlights via spaced repetition, prompting note creation from forgotten material. For books, use Kindle Highlights + Readwise for automatic sync without manual highlight transcription. The pipeline is: Read → Highlight → Readwise sync → Obsidian literature note → Process into permanent note.

evidence_refs.description: Readwise export and Obsidian integration documentation

statement: Logseq namespaced pages use '/' to create hierarchical page relationships without folders, enabling a taxonomy system that maintains Logseq's flat file structure. UNSAID: A page named 'Projects/ACME/Meeting Notes' creates a hierarchical namespace where 'Projects/ACME' is a parent page automatically. In the graph view, namespaced pages cluster together visually. The sidebar shows namespaces as collapsible trees. This is fundamentally different from Obsidian's folder-based hierarchy — Logseq's namespaces are page naming conventions, not file system directories. All files remain in a flat directory structure. The practical advantage: you can reference a namespace parent ([[Projects/ACME]]) to see all child pages as linked references, effectively creating a project hub page automatically. Namespace pages work with Logseq queries for hierarchical filtering. The limitation: namespace depth beyond 3 levels becomes unwieldy, and renaming a parent namespace requires manually updating all child page names.

Obsidian vault migration from Notion involves a two-phase process: export and restructure, because Notion's hierarchical database model maps poorly to Obsidian's flat file model. UNSAID: Notion exports to markdown + CSV but produces deeply nested folder structures (sub-pages become sub-folders) and database items become individual files in a folder. The Notion-to-Obsidian community conversion tools (notion-to-obsidian-converter) handle basic link conversion but struggle with Notion-specific features: formulas become static text, rollups disappear, relations become plain text instead of links. Embedded databases don't convert. Post-migration steps: 1) Flatten folder hierarchy using scripts, 2) Convert Notion-style [[page title]] links to Obsidian format, 3) Replace table databases with Dataview queries over frontmatter-rich notes, 4) Rebuild relation-style connections as wikilinks. Budget 2-4 hours of manual cleanup per 100 Notion pages for a thorough migration. Accept that some Notion features (gallery view, board view) have no direct Obsidian equivalent.

Obsidian's commitment to plain markdown files stored locally is its primary architectural differentiator from SaaS PKM tools, ensuring future-proofing against product discontinuation. UNSAID: The PKM tool graveyard (Roam Research price shock, Notion rate limiting, Evernote ownership changes, Notion AI pricing tiers) has made many knowledge workers anxious about vendor lock-in. Obsidian's local markdown storage means your notes are readable by any text editor that has ever existed and ever will exist — there is no migration risk. The Obsidian business model (one-time purchase for commercial use, optional Sync/Publish subscriptions) doesn't create pressure to add value-destroying monetization. The community plugin ecosystem (1000+ plugins) means Obsidian evolves faster than a single company product could. The risk profile: Obsidian the company could disappear, but the vault remains 100% functional as local markdown files with any compatible tool. This philosophy has made Obsidian the preferred tool for researchers, writers, and developers who treat their notes as long-term intellectual assets.

Obsidian periodic notes system (daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, annual) creates a temporal scaffolding for reflection and review that connects individual journal entries to higher-level patterns. UNSAID: The Periodic Notes plugin (community) extends the Daily Notes core plugin to weekly/monthly/quarterly/yearly cadences, each with its own template. The key linking pattern: each daily note links up to the weekly note via [[{{date:gggg-[W]WW}}]], weekly notes link to monthly, creating a rollup hierarchy. Dataview queries in weekly/monthly notes can aggregate tasks completed, habits logged, and books read from child period notes. This enables retrospective analysis: a monthly note Dataview query shows all fleeting ideas captured in daily notes that month, prompting permanent note creation from overlooked insights. The calendar plugin (community) provides a visual monthly grid linking to daily and weekly notes, making navigation intuitive. Critical: templates for periodic notes should include reflection prompts (what went well, what to change) to make reviews actionable rather than just archival.

Logseq's whiteboard feature (Excalidraw integration) creates diagrams stored as block properties rather than separate files, making them inaccessible to external tools and causing export issues. UNSAID: Logseq Whiteboards store diagram data as a JSON-encoded block property value within the page's EBNF format. This means the diagram is not a standalone Excalidraw file — it cannot be opened in Excalidraw.com or other Excalidraw clients directly. Exporting diagrams requires using Logseq's export function, which creates PNG/SVG but loses editability. For diagrams that need to be shared or edited outside Logseq, use the Excalidraw Obsidian plugin instead, which stores diagrams as actual .excalidraw files that are portable. The Logseq whiteboard feature is best used for thinking-on-canvas during research sessions, not for shareable architecture diagrams. Block references from whiteboards to Logseq pages work bidirectionally, making it useful for concept mapping tied to written notes.

Obsidian as a CRM (Customer Relationship Management) tool uses contact notes with YAML frontmatter properties, Dataview for contact lookups, and periodic notes integration for meeting tracking. UNSAID: Create a /Contacts/ folder with a template including frontmatter: company, role, email, last_contact, status. Each meeting generates a dated interaction note linked to the contact note. Dataview queries in a 'Contacts Dashboard' note can surface: contacts not reached in 90+ days, upcoming follow-ups (filter by next_contact date in frontmatter), and deal pipeline status. This personal CRM approach works well for solo freelancers and consultants with <500 contacts. Beyond that, dedicated CRM software's search, deduplication, and team features become necessary. The obsidian-full-calendar plugin can show follow-up reminders from contact frontmatter dates on a calendar view. For business development, link contact notes to opportunity notes using bidirectional wikilinks to track relationship-to-deal pathways.

evidence_refs.description: Dataview queries for CRM-style contact management

statement: Logseq's PDF annotation feature stores annotations in the graph database rather than inside the PDF file, making annotations invisible to PDF readers and impossible to export as standard PDF annotations. UNSAID: When you annotate a PDF in Logseq (highlight, comment), these annotations are stored as Logseq blocks with position metadata, not as PDF annotation objects embedded in the file. This means: opening the same PDF in Zotero, Preview, or Acrobat shows no annotations; sharing the PDF with a colleague loses all annotations; moving the PDF to a different location breaks the annotation link. For research workflows requiring portable annotations, use Zotero (stores annotations in PDF) or Hypothesis (web-based with export). Logseq PDF annotations excel for personal literature notes that stay within Logseq — the block reference system allows citing specific highlighted passages in other notes. The hlsm:: (highlights) property on literature note pages stores the annotation blocks automatically.

Obsidian QuickAdd plugin enables one-hotkey note creation from predefined templates with dynamic inputs, dramatically accelerating capture workflows. UNSAID: QuickAdd's four modes — Capture, Template, Macro, Multi — cover the spectrum from appending text to a specific file (capture mode, ideal for inbox) to chaining multiple actions (macro mode). The Template choice command presents a menu of templates to choose from via fuzzy search, then creates and opens the note immediately. For capture workflows: set up a QuickAdd capture that appends timestamped entries to a daily inbox note without opening it, allowing rapid capture without losing focus. The Macro mode enables 'smart' workflows: prompt for input, create a note, open it, move cursor to specific position, then run a Templater script. QuickAdd's choice format picker allows creating notes in dynamic paths (e.g., 'Meetings/{{DATE:YYYY-MM}}/{{VALUE}}') where VALUE is the prompt response. This is faster than manual new note creation for structured note types.

Logseq's open source model (AGPL license) with a freemium SaaS component creates a sustainable PKM tool with community-driven development, contrasting with VC-backed tools that optimize for growth over user value. UNSAID: Logseq core is AGPL-licensed, meaning any hosted derivative must also be open source — protecting against proprietary forks. The Logseq team (based in SF with international contributors) monetizes via Logseq Sync (cloud sync) while keeping the desktop/mobile apps free. The DB Logseq version introduces a SaaS sync model that makes the business more sustainable but reduces the fully-offline appeal. Community development has contributed major features: whiteboards, PDF highlights, and many query improvements originated as community contributions. The practical implication: Logseq's development roadmap is influenced by community votes, meaning power user features get prioritized over mainstream appeal. For users who need guaranteed enterprise SLAs, Obsidian's commercial licensing model may be more appropriate.

evidence_refs.description: Logseq open source repository and license

statement: Personal knowledge management system audit using Dataview queries identifies orphaned notes, under-linked hubs, and stale content that degrades vault quality over time. UNSAID: Run quarterly PKM audits with these Dataview queries: 1) Orphaned notes (no incoming or outgoing links): TABLE file.name WHERE length(file.inlinks) = 0 AND length(file.outlinks) = 0 — these are dead ends that should be linked or deleted. 2) Over-linked hubs (potential MOCs): TABLE file.name, length(file.inlinks) as inlinks WHERE length(file.inlinks) > 15 ORDER BY inlinks DESC — these are the most important notes by connectivity. 3) Stale notes (not modified in 180 days): TABLE file.name, file.mtime WHERE file.mtime < date(today) - dur(180 days) — candidates for archival or update. 4) Notes without tags: TABLE file.name WHERE length(file.tags) = 0 — missing classification. Run these audits before adding new notes to prevent vault entropy. A healthy vault has 70%+ of notes with at least one incoming link.

evidence_refs.description: Dataview page metadata for vault auditing

statement: Obsidian Custom CSS snippets can break the entire Obsidian UI if they target core CSS variables incorrectly, causing invisible text or unclickable interface elements. UNSAID: Obsidian uses CSS custom properties (--color-base-100 through --color-base-100) for theming. Custom snippets that override these variables with fixed colors instead of relative values break dark/light mode switching — the vault looks correct in one mode but unreadable in the other. CSS snippets run in the same scope as Obsidian's own styles with no sandboxing — a typo can cause widespread layout breakage. Safe snippet development: use the Obsidian developer console (Ctrl+Shift+I) to inspect elements and test CSS live before saving to snippets. Enable snippets one at a time to identify which one causes issues. Use the Style Settings plugin (community) which provides UI controls for theme variables rather than raw CSS editing. Always back up working snippets before editing, as there's no undo for saved snippet changes.

Setting up Obsidian for a team with shared vault requires careful access control design because Obsidian has no native permission system — all vault users have full read/write access to all notes. UNSAID: For team vaults, implement access control at the file system level: use gitignore patterns to exclude personal notes from the shared repository, or maintain separate personal and team vaults with a reference system linking between them. The Remotely Save plugin (community) or Obsidian Sync (for teams) handles multi-user sync but without per-note access control. For editorial workflows, establish naming conventions: prefix WIP notes with '_draft-' that are gitignored, only committed notes are 'published' to the team. Use git branches for major collaborative documents under review. Frontmatter 'owner' and 'status' fields with Dataview provide visibility into who's working on what. For larger teams, consider Notion (hierarchical permissions) or Confluence (proper access control) instead of Obsidian's flat shared vault model.

Obsidian AI integration via local LLM (Ollama) or API-based models (OpenAI, Anthropic) enables in-vault AI assistance while maintaining the local-first philosophy through the Smart Connections or Text Generator plugins. UNSAID: Smart Connections plugin indexes the entire vault using embeddings (locally via Transformers.js or via OpenAI API) to find semantically related notes — more powerful than Obsidian's keyword-based backlinks. The Text Generator plugin calls LLM APIs to generate text inline, summarize notes, or suggest connections, using the current note's content as context. Privacy consideration: API-based embedding sends note content to external servers — for sensitive vaults, use local models via Ollama (nomic-embed-text for embeddings, llama3 for generation). The practical limitation: local models on consumer hardware are 3-10x slower than API models for real-time assistance. The Smart Connections canvas feature creates a visual map of semantically similar notes — an AI-assisted alternative to manual graph curation. Combine with Templater for AI-enhanced note creation workflows.

evidence_refs.description: Smart Connections plugin for AI-powered Obsidian

statement: Obsidian Excalidraw plugin stores diagrams as markdown files with embedded SVG and JSON, enabling version-controllable visual diagrams linked to note context. UNSAID: Excalidraw files in Obsidian are .md files with frontmatter containing the drawing JSON — they render as diagrams in preview mode and as editable drawings in edit mode. This dual-nature means the file can be wiklinked from notes, appears in graph view with backlinks, and is version-controlled by git with diffable JSON changes. Create Excalidraw templates for common diagram types (architecture diagrams, flowcharts, mind maps) to maintain visual consistency across the vault. The 'embed drawings' feature allows inserting an Excalidraw diagram into any note using ![[diagram.excalidraw]] with optional size parameters. For system design documentation, combine a written architecture note with embedded Excalidraw diagrams — when the diagram updates, the embedding note automatically shows the new version. Scripted Excalidraw (via the script API) enables generating diagrams programmatically from vault data.

Logseq's 'preferred' format setting (markdown vs org-mode) cannot be changed after a graph is created without risk of corrupting existing notes that use the original format. UNSAID: When creating a new Logseq graph, choose between Markdown and Org-mode formats — this determines how bullets, headings, and block properties are encoded in files. Changing this setting mid-graph causes Logseq to misinterpret existing files, rendering bullets as raw text or showing malformed outlines. If you accidentally create a graph in org-mode when you wanted markdown (a common mistake for users coming from Emacs), the fix is to export all notes and create a new graph in the correct format, then re-import. For users coming from Obsidian, choose Markdown format to maintain file compatibility with Obsidian (which also uses Markdown). Org-mode format is recommended only for Emacs/Org-roam users who need bidirectional compatibility with their Emacs workflow.

Book notes system in Obsidian uses a structured template combining reading status tracking, key insights, and connection to other vault notes for building a personal library of synthesized knowledge. UNSAID: Book note template includes: frontmatter (title, author, year, status: reading/complete/abandoned, rating, finished_date, tags), then sections: Synopsis (100-word summary in your own words), Key Concepts (bulleted insights you want to remember), Memorable Quotes (verbatim with page numbers), Questions Raised (unresolved questions the book prompted), Connected Notes (manual wikilinks to related permanent notes). The crucial step most skip: write the Synopsis BEFORE adding quotes — writing in your own words reveals whether you actually understood the book. Track reading metrics in a Reading Log note that uses Dataview to query all books with status:complete and calculate yearly totals. For Kindle books, Readwise sync auto-populates the Quotes section, making manual transcription unnecessary.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian templates for structured note creation

statement: Obsidian's search functionality uses simple keyword matching and does not support semantic similarity search without plugin additions, making large vaults harder to mine for relevant content. UNSAID: Built-in Obsidian search supports: exact phrase ("quoted"), path filter (path:folder/), tag filter (tag:#topic), property filter (property:value), boolean operators (AND, OR, NOT), and regex. It does NOT support semantic search — searching for 'habit building' won't surface notes about 'routine formation.' This limitation becomes significant beyond 500 notes when you can't recall exact terminology. Community solutions: Smart Connections (embedding-based semantic search), Omnisearch (BM25 full-text search, much faster than default for large vaults), or vector database integrations. The Omnisearch plugin is the first upgrade to make for vaults over 1000 notes — it indexes all content including PDF text extracted from embedded PDFs, enabling full-text search across attachments that built-in search cannot access.

Logseq's task management with /TODO /DOING /DONE workflow states combined with SCHEDULED:: and DEADLINE:: properties creates an Org-mode-style GTD system within the outline-based editor. UNSAID: Logseq tasks use keyword states that can be customized in settings (default: TODO → DOING → DONE → LATER → NOW → WAITING). The /keyword slash command inserts tasks, and clicking cycles through states. SCHEDULED:: [[2024-03-15]] and DEADLINE:: [[2024-03-20]] properties make tasks appear in the agenda queries for those dates. The built-in '(({query (task TODO DOING)}))' query creates a global task board. For GTD (Getting Things Done) implementation: use NEXT state (custom) for next actions, @context tags for context filtering, and areas of focus as parent blocks. The critical Logseq advantage over Obsidian Tasks: tasks are just outline blocks with a keyword — they naturally inherit context from their parent bullets, making project-task hierarchies implicit. Scheduled tasks appear in Logseq's sidebar agenda without additional plugin.

evidence_refs.description: Logseq tasks and TODO states documentation

statement: Combining Obsidian with Anki flashcard system via the Obsidian-to-Anki plugin transforms note content into spaced repetition decks, connecting active recall to the knowledge base. UNSAID: The Obsidian-to-Anki plugin (community) converts specially formatted Obsidian notes into Anki cards using either inline format (TARGET::ANSWER on consecutive lines) or cloze format ({{c1::hidden text}}). Cards are created/updated in Anki on demand, with a bidirectional link back to the source Obsidian note. The workflow: write notes normally, then identify key concepts to memorize and wrap them in cloze markup. The Spaced Repetition plugin (community) provides in-Obsidian flashcards without requiring Anki, but lacks Anki's mature algorithm and cross-device sync. Critical insight: only create flashcards for facts you need fast recall of — not for understanding-based knowledge. Procedural knowledge (how to do X) is better learned by doing than by Anki drilling. For language learning, AnkiConnect (Anki addon) combined with Obsidian-to-Anki creates vocabulary cards directly from reading notes.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian to Anki plugin for spaced repetition

statement: Migrating from Roam Research to Obsidian requires handling Roam's block-level UUIDs, daily note format differences, and nested bullet structure that doesn't map directly to Obsidian's flat file model. UNSAID: Export Roam as JSON (more complete than Markdown export which loses some block properties). Use the Roam-to-Obsidian conversion tools (roam-to-md or roam-to-obsidian) to convert JSON to individual markdown files. Post-conversion issues: 1) Roam's ((block-uuid)) references become orphaned — they need manual resolution or conversion to page links since Obsidian doesn't support block IDs in the same way. 2) Roam's #tags convert to Obsidian tags but #[[multi-word tags]] need the spaces handled. 3) Daily notes in Roam use 'January 15th, 2024' format while Obsidian typically uses 'YYYY-MM-DD' — configure Obsidian daily notes format to match Roam's format during migration. Budget significant cleanup time: 50-100 Roam pages may need 1-2 hours of manual refinement for full structural integrity.

evidence_refs.description: Official Obsidian Roam Research import guide

statement: Obsidian Map of Content (MOC) notes serve as navigational hubs that curate and connect related notes without imposing hierarchical folder structure, enabling topic exploration at multiple abstraction levels. UNSAID: An MOC is a note whose primary content is links to other notes on a topic, with brief contextual annotations for each link. MOCs differ from folders: a note can appear in multiple MOCs (e.g., a note about 'habit stacking' appears in both the [[Productivity MOC]] and [[Psychology MOC]]), while folders enforce single placement. Create MOCs reactively, not preemptively — start an MOC when you have 10+ notes on a topic and navigation becomes difficult. Three types of MOC content: entrance MOC (high-level topic overview, links to sub-MOCs), contextual MOC (curated list for a specific use case), and brainstorm MOC (exploratory connections during active research). The graph view becomes most useful when MOCs are properly developed — they appear as high-connectivity hubs connecting thematic neighborhoods.

evidence_refs.description: Maps of Content concept by Nick Milo

statement: Obsidian and Logseq both suffer from 'collector's fallacy' — the tendency to treat capturing and organizing information as a substitute for thinking, resulting in large vaults with low intellectual output. UNSAID: The collector's fallacy manifests as: spending hours tagging and reorganizing notes instead of processing them into original ideas; bookmarking articles 'to read later' that never get read; building elaborate Dataview dashboards instead of completing projects. Research on learning (Craik and Lockhart, levels of processing theory) shows that deep processing (elaborating, connecting, generating questions) creates durable memory, while shallow processing (copying text, highlighting) creates the illusion of learning. The countermeasure: impose a processing ratio — for every hour of capturing, spend at least equal time generating output (writing, projects, decisions). Logseq's journal-based workflow naturally forces engagement with captured content through daily review, while Obsidian's folder-based model can become a passive archive without deliberate process discipline.

evidence_refs.description: The Collector's Fallacy by Christian Tietze

statement: The Obsidian vs Logseq choice should be driven by primary use case: Logseq excels for daily journaling and networked thinking with block granularity, while Obsidian excels for structured writing, publication, and plugin-extensible workflows. UNSAID: The fundamental architectural difference: Logseq is an outliner where blocks are the atomic unit; Obsidian is a note editor where files are the atomic unit. Block-level granularity in Logseq enables finer-grained connections and queries but creates workflow friction for long-form writing (entire essays in a single outline bullet structure is unwieldy). Obsidian's file-level granularity maps well to documents, articles, and structured reference material. Many power users maintain both: Logseq for daily capture and thinking (replacing journaling), Obsidian for reference library and writing projects. The file compatibility makes this dual-tool approach viable — both tools can read each other's markdown files (with minor formatting differences). Choose based on: daily journal users → Logseq; writers and researchers → Obsidian; GTD-heavy task managers → Logseq; document-centric teams → Obsidian.

Obsidian Kanban plugin transforms any note into a project board with swimlane columns (Backlog, In Progress, Done), providing a visual project management view while keeping data in editable markdown. UNSAID: Kanban boards in Obsidian are stored as regular markdown with specific heading and bullet conventions that the plugin renders visually. This means the board is fully editable in text mode and version-controlled by git. Cards support: due dates (rendered with countdown), tags, linked notes (click to open), and cover images. The critical limitation: Obsidian Kanban boards are single-file, single-user — no real-time collaboration. For team kanban boards, use a dedicated tool (Linear, Notion, Jira). The best use case is personal project management where cards link to detailed project notes — click a card to open the full project specification note. Archiving completed cards to the Done column keeps history; enable 'List items with dates in the future' to show upcoming deadlines as calendar items if using Calendar plugin.

Logseq's property system with typed properties (using property:: value syntax) creates structured data within the outline, enabling query-based knowledge retrieval across the entire graph. UNSAID: Logseq properties follow the 'property-name:: value' format on a block's first child line. Properties with specific naming conventions are parsed as types: [[page reference]] values create graph links, #tags create tag relationships, and numeric values support range queries. The critical design insight: properties are per-block, not per-page — you can have different blocks in the same page with different property sets. For database-like use cases, create 'entry' pages where each page represents a record: a book database has one page per book with title::, author::, status::, rating:: properties, then query all books with '{{query (and (property status
)}})'. This block-database pattern is more flexible than Obsidian's frontmatter (which is per-file only) but less structured than Notion's actual database columns.

Building a habit tracking system in Obsidian using daily note templates with checkboxes and Dataview aggregation creates a quantified self dashboard without external apps. UNSAID: Daily note template includes a 'Habits' section with checkbox items for each tracked habit. Dataview query in a 'Habit Dashboard' note counts checked vs total days for each habit using: TABLE without id dateformat(file.day, "ddd MM-DD") as Date, ... FROM "Daily Notes" WHERE file.day >= date(today) - dur(30 days). The critical design: use consistent habit names across all templates — if you rename a habit, Dataview can't match across old/new names. Track streaks by querying consecutive days without a missed checkbox. For habits that aren't binary (e.g., '30-minute walk' could be 15 minutes), use a numeric property instead of a checkbox: walk:: 30 in frontmatter with Dataview querying the sum. The Obsidian Tracker plugin (community) provides better visualization (charts, calendars) than raw Dataview tables for habit data.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian Tracker plugin for habit and data visualization

statement: Logseq sync (official paid service) uses E2E encryption like Obsidian Sync but its sync conflicts are resolved at the block level rather than file level, creating partial-merge situations that can leave pages in inconsistent states. UNSAID: When two devices edit different blocks in the same Logseq page simultaneously, sync attempts to merge at block granularity — a technical advantage over file-level merge conflicts. However, the block-level merge algorithm has edge cases: reordering blocks on two devices simultaneously can result in duplicated or out-of-order blocks that appear as if the page has extra content. These conflicts don't show obvious conflict markers — you'll notice the page has duplicate sections. Resolution requires manual inspection and deletion of duplicate blocks. For DB Logseq (upcoming version), sync is redesigned as a first-class feature with better conflict resolution using CRDT (Conflict-free Replicated Data Types), which should resolve these issues. Until DB Logseq stabilizes, using git-based sync with manual pull before editing on each device is more reliable for frequent multi-device users.

Obsidian as a second brain for software developers combines code snippets (with syntax highlighting), architecture decision records (ADRs), and API documentation notes linked to project notes. UNSAID: Developer-specific vault setup: /Projects/<project-name>/ for each active project, /ADRs/ for architecture decisions (template: status, context, decision, consequences), /Snippets/ for reusable code with language tags, /Docs/ for external API documentation summaries. The Code Styler plugin improves code block rendering with line numbers and syntax highlighting. Use Dataview to surface: all ADRs with status:proposed across all projects (pending review), all code snippets tagged with a specific language, and all project notes modified in the last week. GitHub integration via the GitHub Publisher plugin (community) can sync specific Obsidian notes to a GitHub wiki or README, enabling vault-to-documentation publishing. For architecture diagrams linked to ADRs, Mermaid.js syntax renders natively in Obsidian preview mode without plugins.

evidence_refs.description: Mermaid.js for diagrams in Markdown documentation

statement: Logseq Flashcards (native feature) transforms any block into a spaced repetition card without external plugins, using #card tag and the |separator| or parent/child block structure. UNSAID: Logseq's built-in flashcard system (Settings > Enable Flashcards) uses two formats: 1) Parent block as question, first child as answer: write the question as a block, indent the answer — add #card to the parent block. 2) Cloze format: add {{cloze text to hide}} around the answer within a single block. Review mode is accessed via Flashcards button in sidebar, showing due cards using spaced repetition algorithm (SM2-inspired). The critical difference from Anki: Logseq flashcards only work within Logseq — no multi-device sync beyond Logseq sync, no AnkiDroid, no audio. For serious language learning or medical flashcards needing cross-device mobile review, export to Anki via the Logseq-Anki-Sync plugin (community). Native flashcards excel for quick knowledge testing during a reading session without context-switching to another app.

Obsidian Dataview 'dv.pages()' in DataviewJS iterates ALL pages in the vault for each call, causing severe performance degradation when called multiple times in a single note with a large vault. UNSAID: DataviewJS `dv.pages()` loads page metadata for every matching page in memory for each call. In a note with 5 DataviewJS blocks each calling dv.pages(), a vault with 3000 notes results in 15,000 page loads per render. The symptoms: note takes 3-5 seconds to open, CPU spikes when switching to the note. Optimization strategies: 1) Use DQL (non-JS Dataview) for simple queries — it uses the index cache more efficiently. 2) For DataviewJS, cache the result: `const pages = dv.pages(source); const books = pages.where(p => p.type === 'book')` (one load, multiple filters). 3) Scope queries with specific folder paths: `dv.pages('
')` instead of `dv.pages()`. 4) Avoid DataviewJS in daily notes templates that open on every HA startup — the vault-wide query runs on each daily note view.

Obsidian and Logseq can coexist pointing at the same folder of markdown files, allowing users to use both tools interchangeably with careful plugin configuration to avoid format conflicts. UNSAID: Open the same directory as both an Obsidian vault and a Logseq graph. Both tools will read the same markdown files. Conflicts arise from: 1) Logseq adds 'id:: UUID' properties to blocks — configure Logseq to disable block ID insertion to avoid polluting files. 2) Logseq uses 'pages/' subfolder while Obsidian uses flat structure — configure Logseq's graph to use the vault root, not a pages subdirectory. 3) Logseq's daily notes use 'journals/' folder by default — either accept this folder in your Obsidian vault or configure both to use the same naming. The practical use case: use Logseq on mobile for quick capture (better outline editing on touch) and Obsidian on desktop for long-form writing (better editor). Test the setup with a sample vault before migrating a production vault.

Building a weekly review system in Obsidian using automation from Templater and Dataview creates a reflective practice that surfaces incomplete work, identifies patterns, and sets priorities. UNSAID: Weekly note template auto-populates via Templater: 1) Incomplete tasks from the past week using Tasks plugin query, 2) New notes created this week via Dataview (file.ctime filter), 3) Goals defined in monthly note as embedded block. The review process: Monday morning open weekly note, review previous week's incomplete tasks (carry forward or abandon with note), scan new notes created and link them to existing MOCs if unlinked, set 3 weekly priorities in the 'This Week' section. The weekly note also serves as a productivity journal — write 3 bullets about what went well and one about what to improve. Dataview tracks the review streak: a query counts weekly notes with 'reviewed:: true' frontmatter set during the review, creating accountability data visible in monthly notes. The Friday evening alternative to Monday morning review reduces weekend task anxiety.

evidence_refs.description: Templater system functions for weekly review automation

statement: The emergence of AI-powered note-taking tools (Notion AI, Mem AI, Reflect) applies pressure on Obsidian/Logseq to integrate AI, but the local-first community values privacy and control over convenience, creating a bifurcation in the PKM market. UNSAID: AI note tools offer automatic linking, smart summaries, and natural language queries that require centralized servers for model inference. This directly conflicts with Obsidian's local-first philosophy. The local AI compromise (Ollama + Smart Connections) requires hardware that most users don't have (8GB+ RAM for acceptable performance). The resulting market bifurcation: users who accept cloud AI get dramatically better AI features (Mem AI's automatic connection surface, Notion AI's one-click summaries), users who prefer local control use Obsidian/Logseq with limited AI. The strategic bet of local-first tools is that edge AI will improve faster than expected, eventually making local models competitive with cloud models. Until then, Obsidian's advantage remains non-AI: longevity, portability, and the absence of subscription dependency.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian blog on product direction and AI strategy

statement: Obsidian note naming conventions significantly impact long-term vault usability — event-based naming ('2024-03-15 meeting with CEO') vs concept-based naming ('CEO Meeting Template') serve different retrieval patterns. UNSAID: Time-based names (ISO date prefix) sort chronologically and enable date-range filtering in Dataview, ideal for meetings, journal entries, and project logs. Concept-based names ('Atomic Habits - Book Notes') are easier to remember and wiklink with [[]] without typing dates. Use a hybrid: permanent notes use concept names (recall-based), fleeting notes and logs use date prefixes (chronological). Avoid names that describe what a note is ('Book Summary of X') — instead use names that describe the idea ('Reading X changes how I think about Y'). Very long note names (>60 characters) get truncated in sidebar and hover previews. The '!' prefix convention for MOC notes ('!Topic Overview') makes them sort to the top alphabetically within a folder, improving visual navigation.

Digital garden publishing from Obsidian using Quartz static site generator transforms a private vault into a public knowledge base with Obsidian-specific features preserved. UNSAID: Quartz 4 (current version) is a Next.js-based SSG that understands Obsidian markdown: wikilinks convert to HTML links, backlinks appear at page bottom, graph view renders as interactive D3 visualization on the website. Setup: clone Quartz repo, configure quartz.config.ts with your content settings, copy vault notes to the 'content/' folder (or symlink), run 'npx quartz build --serve' for local preview, deploy to Cloudflare Pages/GitHub Pages. The selective publishing pattern: add 'public: true' frontmatter to notes you want published, configure Quartz to only include notes with this property. Notes without the property remain private in your vault. Custom CSS classes in Obsidian notes transfer to Quartz for specialized callout styling. The Obsidian Digital Garden plugin (community) offers an alternative deployment path with simpler setup but less customization than Quartz.

evidence_refs.description: Quartz static site generator for Obsidian documentation

statement: Obsidian's link resolution is case-insensitive on case-insensitive file systems (macOS, Windows) but case-sensitive on Linux, creating vault portability issues for users who sync between platforms. UNSAID: On macOS, [[My Note]] and [[my note]] resolve to the same file. On Linux (where the file system is case-sensitive), [[My Note]] and [[my note]] are different files. This means a vault built on macOS with mixed-case wikilinks may have broken links when accessed on a Linux server (e.g., for digital garden publishing via GitHub Actions). Auditing with a script that checks for case-inconsistency between link text and actual filenames prevents this. The Obsidian Linter plugin (community) can enforce consistent link casing across the vault. For team vaults or digital gardens, enforce a consistent naming convention (all lowercase with hyphens, or Title Case consistently) and use the Linter to enforce it at edit time rather than discovering issues during deployment.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian internal links case sensitivity behavior

statement: Logseq's flashcard-driven learning pairs with journal entries for 'learning in public' — writing what you learned in journal entries, tagging key concepts for flashcard creation, and reviewing connections each morning. UNSAID: The daily learning ritual: 1) Morning: review 10-15 flashcards (#card blocks due today) — 5-10 minutes. 2) During day: capture learnings in journal with #card on key facts to memorize. 3) Evening: link new journal learnings to existing concept pages, creating backlinks that surface related prior knowledge. The difference from passive reading: creating a flashcard requires reformulating knowledge in your own words (elaborative encoding), which dramatically improves retention compared to re-reading. The Logseq graph grows organically: new journal entries citing existing concept pages strengthen the link count on those pages, gradually revealing which concepts are truly central to your thinking. After 3-6 months of consistent practice, the graph's connected components visually show your core knowledge domains and gaps.

Obsidian plugin development setup requires understanding the Electron-based architecture and TypeScript compilation pipeline that converts plugin source to the 'main.js' Obsidian loads. UNSAID: Obsidian plugins are TypeScript modules compiled to a single main.js + manifest.json + optionally styles.css. Use the official obsidian-sample-plugin as starter. The development loop: run 'npm run dev' to watch-compile TypeScript, enable the plugin in Obsidian settings, then use Ctrl+Shift+I (developer console) for debugging. For hot reload without restart, install the Hot Reload community plugin. The Obsidian API (obsidian.d.ts) documents all available interfaces — implement Plugin as your entry point with onload() and onunload() lifecycle hooks. Critical: plugins run in the renderer process (not main), meaning they have DOM access but limited Node.js access — file system operations must use the Vault API (this.app.vault) rather than Node 'fs'. The 'requestUrl' function is the correct way to make HTTP requests from plugins (works on both desktop and mobile), not fetch() or http.request().

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian plugin development getting started guide

statement: Obsidian vault backup strategies that rely solely on the local drive create unacceptable data loss risk — without offsite backup, drive failure loses the entire knowledge base. UNSAID: A PKM vault represents years of intellectual work and is irreplaceable — this warrants a more aggressive backup strategy than typical documents. The 3-2-1 backup rule (3 copies, 2 media types, 1 offsite) applied to Obsidian: Local vault (SSD), Time Machine or local backup drive (second media), Obsidian Sync or git remote (offsite). Many users discover they have no backup only after a drive failure. obsidian-git auto-push provides the offsite backup as a side effect of version control. Critical failure mode: users who rely on iCloud Drive as their only backup discover that iCloud silently 'optimizes' storage by removing local files — if Obsidian opens a note before iCloud downloads it, the file appears empty and the auto-save overwrites the cloud version with an empty file. Always verify iCloud Drive is in 'Download Always' mode for your vault folder.

Logseq templates reduce friction for structured capture by auto-populating standard block structures when creating certain page types, triggered by the /template command or auto-apply on page title patterns. UNSAID: Access Logseq templates via /template (shows all saved templates) or configure auto-apply in Settings > Templates > Auto-apply templates by pattern (e.g., any page with 'Meeting' in the name auto-applies the Meeting template). Template syntax uses {today} for current date, {yesterday}, {current-page} for the page name. Unlike Obsidian's Templater which runs JavaScript, Logseq templates are static text — no dynamic content generation except built-in date variables. For complex template logic, use a slash command that fires a Logseq macro or script. The meeting template pattern: Project::, Attendees::, Action Items:: (as a nested list with owners and due dates), Next Steps:: — these become queryable properties across all meeting notes. Recurring meeting templates can auto-generate based on the journal date, prefilling the recurring agenda items.

Obsidian integration with external task managers (Todoist, Things 3, OmniFocus) via plugins creates a bidirectional flow where project notes live in Obsidian but actionable tasks live in the dedicated task manager. UNSAID: The Todoist Sync plugin (community) syncs Todoist tasks to Obsidian notes using task ID metadata. Tasks created in Todoist appear in Obsidian project notes; completing them in either app syncs the status. The natural split: Obsidian holds the 'why' and context (project specs, reference material, decisions), Todoist/Things holds the 'what and when' (scheduled actions with reminders). This separation of concerns prevents Obsidian from becoming a task manager (which it does poorly due to no mobile push notifications or calendar sync) while keeping context in the writing environment. For Things 3 (Mac only), the 'thingskit' URL scheme allows creating tasks from Obsidian link callbacks. The obsidian-todoist-plugin creates task queries that render Todoist task lists inline in Obsidian notes, enabling a project note that shows both documentation and outstanding tasks.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian Todoist plugin for task manager integration

statement: The true competitive moat of a well-maintained Obsidian or Logseq vault is not the tool but the accumulated personal knowledge graph — connections between ideas that take years to build and cannot be transferred or replicated. UNSAID: Unlike files (which can be migrated) or code (which can be rewritten), the link density and personal relevance of a mature PKM vault is the user's unique intellectual fingerprint. A 5000-note vault with 10,000 meaningful links represents years of reading, thinking, and connecting — switching tools means either losing this connectivity or spending months re-establishing it. This is why PKM tool choice should optimize for the tool you'll actually use consistently for 5+ years, not the tool with the best features today. The retention effect compounds: each note added to a well-maintained vault becomes more valuable because it gains connections to years of accumulated context. A sparse, perfectly-organized 200-note vault is less valuable than a dense, imperfectly-organized 2000-note vault with 5000 meaningful connections.

evidence_refs.description: Digital garden philosophy and history by Maggie Appleton

statement: Obsidian for language learning combines vocabulary notes, grammar reference, reading logs, and Anki integration to create an immersive study environment that connects input to structured practice. UNSAID: Vocabulary note structure: the target word as filename, with frontmatter: language, word, reading (pronunciation), definition, example_sentence, source. A Dataview query in a Language Dashboard shows all vocabulary by source and recency. Link vocabulary notes to the text you encountered them in (reading log notes) for context reinforcement. Grammar notes use MOC structure — a Japanese Grammar MOC links to individual pattern notes, each with multiple example sentences. The Anki sync exports vocabulary and grammar pattern notes to flashcard decks. The reading log tracks immersion time — Dataview can calculate total hours and words per day for accountability. For character-based languages (Chinese, Japanese), the obsidian-cjk-breaker plugin improves search by treating each character as a searchable unit rather than requiring exact multi-character input.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian language learning vault setups in community

statement: Obsidian note transclusion using the '![[note]]' embed syntax creates composable documents where sections of notes appear in multiple contexts, enabling a single-source-of-truth approach for reference content.
