UNSAID: Embedding syntax options: '![[note]]' embeds entire note, '![[note#heading]]' embeds from a specific heading to the next heading, '![[note#^blockid]]' embeds a specific paragraph (requires ^blockid marker in source). This enables: a weekly report note that embeds the current week's project status sections from individual project notes, a dashboard note that embeds key metrics from multiple analysis notes, a course outline that embeds curriculum sections from topic notes. Critical limitation: embedded content is read-only in the note where it's embedded — to edit, you must navigate to the source note. Embeds don't render in plain markdown viewers (GitHub, most editors) — they show as raw wikilink syntax. For publishing with Quartz, embeds render correctly in the HTML output. Deeply nested embeds (note A embeds note B which embeds note C) render but can cause performance issues in preview mode.

Logseq's performance degrades significantly with graphs containing more than 5,000 pages, manifesting as slow graph view rendering, delayed query results, and sluggish block search. UNSAID: Logseq's graph database stores all block metadata in memory for fast querying. Above 5,000 pages, the in-memory index size causes 500MB+ memory consumption and noticeable load delays on startup. The graph view (visual network) becomes unusable above 2,000 nodes with the default force-directed layout. Mitigation: split a large graph into multiple smaller graphs (personal, work, archive) and use Logseq's linked graphs feature for cross-graph references. For archive content (notes older than 2 years that you rarely access), export to a separate archive graph and remove from the active graph — this reduces the active graph size without losing historical content. The DB Logseq version (upcoming) uses SQLite for storage rather than in-memory Datascript, which should alleviate this scaling issue.

evidence_refs.description: Logseq community discussion on performance with large graphs

statement: Obsidian callout blocks provide a CSS-styled way to create attention-drawing information blocks (notes, warnings, tips, examples) that render in both Obsidian and Obsidian Publish without plugins. UNSAID: Callout syntax: '> [!type] Optional Title\n> Content'. Built-in types: note, tip, important, warning, caution, danger, example, quote, info, success, bug, failure, question, abstract, summary. Custom callout types can be defined with CSS snippets that target '.callout[data-callout="custom-type"]'. Callouts can be collapsible with '+' (default expanded) or '-' (default collapsed): '> [!note]+ Collapsible Title'. For documentation notes, callouts visually distinguish: procedures (tip style), warnings (warning style), and examples (example style). In Quartz digital garden publishing, callouts render with the Obsidian-compatible styling automatically. The native markdown alternative (tables/horizontal rules) doesn't carry semantic meaning — callouts communicate importance level, not just visual differentiation.

Recovering a corrupted Obsidian vault after a sync conflict or accidental deletion uses the vault's '.obsidian/' folder to restore plugin settings while recovering note content from backup. UNSAID: The '.obsidian/' directory contains: plugins/ (downloaded plugin code, NOT critical — reinstall plugins), snippets/ (custom CSS — BACK UP), themes/ (downloaded themes — NOT critical), workspace.json (current layout — NOT critical), app.json (app settings), graph.json (graph view settings). If notes are corrupted, the '.obsidian/' folder is typically intact — your plugin configurations are safe even when notes are damaged. Recovery priority: 1) Restore note files from backup (Time Machine, git, Obsidian Sync version history), 2) Verify '.obsidian/' configuration is intact, 3) Reinstall any plugins that were in plugins/ if the folder was corrupted. The Obsidian Sync version history (365 days for Plus tier) lets you restore individual note versions — access via right-clicking a note in the sync panel. For catastrophic failures, git log shows every commit, enabling file-by-file restoration.

Logseq's slash commands (/todo, /now, /later, /template, /date, etc.) provide a command palette for block-level operations that speeds up outlining without leaving the keyboard. UNSAID: The full slash command list includes formatting operations (/bold, /italic, /underline, /code, /highlight), structural operations (/current-time, /yesterday, /tomorrow), and functional operations (/new-template-block, /embed, /tag). The /link-to-page command inserts a page link with fuzzy search, equivalent to typing [[]] but with autocomplete. For custom slash commands, Logseq plugins can register new slash commands via the 'logseq.Editor.registerSlashCommand()' API. The key productivity insight: frequent users memorize 5-10 slash commands that cover 90% of their workflow — /todo, /template, /date, /current-time, and /embed. The slash command palette also serves as a discoverability mechanism for features users don't know exist — occasional exploration of the full command list surfaces useful features.

evidence_refs.description: Logseq keyboard shortcuts and slash commands documentation

statement: Obsidian's Dataview plugin cannot modify notes — it is read-only, meaning Dataview query results cannot be used to write data back to frontmatter or note content without additional plugins. UNSAID: This read-only constraint is intentional: Dataview is a query engine, not an automation engine. For automated frontmatter updates (e.g., update 'last_accessed' date on note open), use the Metadata Menu plugin (community) which adds UI-driven frontmatter editing. For batch frontmatter updates across multiple notes, use the Linter plugin's YAML frontmatter rules. For complex write operations from Dataview-like queries, use DataviewJS in combination with the app.vault.modify() API call — but this bypasses Dataview's read-only design. The MetaEdit plugin (community) adds a command to update frontmatter values in bulk by searching and replacing property values. For automation-style workflows (automatically set status to 'complete' when all tasks are checked), Templater's onFileCreate/onFileChange triggers combined with Vault API write operations provide the reactive update capability Dataview cannot.

Building a personal finance tracking vault in Obsidian uses monthly budget note templates with income/expense tables, Dataview aggregation for trend analysis, and linked notes for transaction context. UNSAID: Monthly budget note template: YAML frontmatter with income, fixed_expenses, variable_expenses as number properties. Transaction detail notes link to monthly note. Dataview query in an Annual Finance Dashboard: TABLE sum(rows.income) as total_income, sum(rows.fixed_expenses) as fixed, sum(rows.variable_expenses) as variable FROM budget_notes GROUP BY dateformat(file.name, 'yyyy'). This approach captures financial data without sending it to cloud services. The practical limitation: manual data entry is error-prone and tedious for people with many transactions. Bank statement CSV import via a Python/Ruby script that creates transaction notes from CSV rows bridges this gap. The real value is the contextual notes: a $500 transaction tagged [[Home Repair]] links to the project note explaining why — context that spreadsheets lack. For tax preparation, a Dataview query filtered by #tax-deductible tag across all expense notes compiles the deductible list automatically.

evidence_refs.description: Dataview queries for financial tracking in Obsidian

statement: Logseq's re-index feature, while useful for fixing display inconsistencies, permanently loses certain customizations and can break custom property types on large graphs. UNSAID: 'Re-index graph' in Logseq Settings rebuilds the Datascript database from raw markdown files. This fixes display issues (phantom pages, broken links) but resets: 1) Custom property value types (configured via the Properties panel UI — these are stored in the DB, not in files), 2) Pinned sidebar items, 3) Open page history. After re-index, you must manually reconfigure any properties with custom types (text, number, date, URL, etc.) since the file doesn't encode property type information — only values. For large graphs (3000+ pages), re-index can take 5-10 minutes during which Logseq is unresponsive. The re-index should be a diagnostic last resort, not a routine maintenance step. Most display issues can be fixed by closing and reopening Logseq or by clearing the cache folder ('.logseq/graphs-cache/').

Obsidian Properties panel (core plugin, 2023.4+) replaces the manual YAML frontmatter editing workflow with a structured form that validates property types and enables auto-complete for existing property names. UNSAID: The Properties panel appears at the top of notes in Reading/Live Preview mode when frontmatter exists. It recognizes property types: text, number, date, datetime, checkbox, list (for array values). Auto-suggest for property names shows all previously used property names across the vault, helping maintain consistent property naming. The critical insight: the Properties panel still stores data as YAML frontmatter — it's a UI overlay, not a different storage system. Existing notes with manually-written YAML frontmatter automatically work with the Properties panel. For large-scale property operations (renaming a property across all vault notes), use Edit > Properties > Rename Property in the command palette — this updates all notes containing that property in one operation. The Properties panel is not visible in Source mode — switch to Reading or Live Preview mode to use it.

Obsidian and Logseq's rapid adoption (combined 1M+ daily users by 2024) represents a broader shift toward personal software ownership — tools you control, that store data you own, without subscription extraction. UNSAID: The PKM movement coincides with growing skepticism about SaaS tools that can change pricing, deprecate features, or shut down (Evernote's near-collapse, Bear's iOS-first limitation). The 'build in public' culture of both tool ecosystems (open plugin APIs, community sharing of vault structures and templates) creates network effects without centralized control. The deeper cultural shift: knowledge workers are treating their note-taking environment as infrastructure — something worth investing in configuring and maintaining, not a commodity tool to switch annually. This investment justification only works if the tool stores data in an open format (markdown), explaining why Notion and Roam's proprietary formats are structural disadvantages in the long-term PKM market. The winner of the PKM market will not be the tool with the best features — it will be the tool users trust most to exist and be compatible with future workflows in 20 years.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian pricing model emphasizing personal control

statement: Obsidian Theme customization via Style Settings plugin enables non-CSS users to configure visual options through a GUI, making theme modification accessible without CSS knowledge. UNSAID: Themes that support Style Settings expose configuration options (color pickers, toggles, sliders) through a structured plugin interface rather than requiring users to edit CSS variables manually. The Style Settings panel appears in Settings after installing the plugin and any compatible theme (Minimal, AnuPpuccin, Shimmering Focus, etc.). For vault-specific styling, create CSS snippets that use the existing theme's CSS variables rather than hardcoding colors — this maintains compatibility when the theme updates. The ':root' selector in snippets applies globally; '.workspace' applies only to the editor area. For high-contrast accessibility needs, the Accessible theme (specifically designed for screen reader and low-vision users) provides WCAG 2.1 AA compliance. Theme previews at obsidian.md/themes show before installing — critical for evaluating reading vs editing experience.

evidence_refs.description: Style Settings plugin for theme configuration

statement: Content creation pipeline using Logseq for research capture and Obsidian for drafting creates a two-phase knowledge work environment optimized for each tool's strengths. UNSAID: Phase 1 (Research/Capture in Logseq): daily journal entries capture readings, ideas, and meeting notes. Concept pages accumulate linked references automatically. Block-level queries identify all captured material on the topic. Phase 2 (Drafting in Obsidian): create a new note in the shared vault folder, transclude relevant Logseq-captured blocks (using copy/paste or manual links since Logseq block IDs aren't portable to Obsidian), write the first draft using accumulated raw material. The handoff between phases requires manual curation — not automatic — which is intentional: the selection process of what to include in the draft IS the synthesis. The Obsidian vault and Logseq graph can share the same folder (with careful config to avoid conflicts), or use a separate 'writing' Obsidian vault that receives curated exports from Logseq. This pipeline mirrors the Zettelkasten source note → literature note → permanent note progression.

evidence_refs.description: Progressive Summarization technique by Tiago Forte

statement: Obsidian's internal link format (wikilinks) is not standard Markdown syntax, causing portability issues when sharing notes with users of other Markdown tools. UNSAID: Standard Markdown uses [text](path/to/file.md) for links; Obsidian uses [[filename]] (wikilink). While Obsidian can display both formats, exported markdown shared via email, GitHub, or other tools renders [[wikilinks]] as plain text rather than clickable links. The Obsidian-to-Markdown compatibility plugin converts wikilinks to standard markdown links on export, but this is a one-way conversion that removes Obsidian-specific features. For teams that share notes via GitHub (markdown files in a repo), configure Obsidian to use markdown links instead of wikilinks (Settings > Files & Links > Use [[wikilinks]]: OFF). This sacrifices some Obsidian convenience (automatic link updating on rename only works reliably with wikilinks) for broader compatibility. Academic users sharing notes with non-Obsidian colleagues should use standard markdown links and avoid Dataview blocks, which render as code fences outside Obsidian.

Logseq tags serve dual purposes: organizational classification (#project, #area) and query filters, but should be kept shallow (max 3 levels) to avoid becoming a maintenance burden. UNSAID: Logseq tags (using # prefix) and page links ([[pagename]]) are equivalent in the query system — both create graph connections. The distinction: tags suggest 'this note has this property' while page links suggest 'this note is about this topic.' Use tags for status/classification (#inbox, #waiting, #someday, #active), use page links for topics ([[marketing]], [[python]]). Tag hierarchies using #parent/child (e.g., #area/health, #area/work) are supported and create grouped filtering in queries. The practical limit: users who create 30+ tags find tag selection becomes an overhead that slows down capture. The 5-10 tag rule: define your core tags upfront (status tags + 5-7 context tags) and resist adding new ones without retiring old ones. Logseq's tag autocomplete helps with typo prevention, but the base discipline must be self-imposed.

Implementing Obsidian for a university course combines lecture notes, paper summaries, assignment tracking, and exam preparation in a connected note system optimized for learning outcomes. UNSAID: Course vault structure: /Courses/<Course-Name>/Lectures/ (numbered files: 01-Introduction.md), /Papers/ (literature notes from readings), /Assignments/ (one note per assignment with due date frontmatter), /Concepts/ (permanent notes for key ideas), /Exam-Prep/ (synthesis notes for exam topics). The learning workflow: during lecture, capture in Logseq or raw Obsidian; after lecture, process into structured notes with concept definitions, examples, and connections to prior knowledge. Papers get 2-level summaries: 200-word synopsis + key contributions. Before exams, create exam prep notes that synthesize 10-20 lectures into testable frameworks — the act of synthesis is more valuable than re-reading notes. Dataview tracks assignment deadlines: TABLE file.name, due WHERE contains(file.path, "Assignments") AND due >= date(today) SORT due. Obsidian's core Tag plugin shows course-wide concept frequency, identifying high-importance themes.

Obsidian community plugin malware risk is non-trivial — because plugins run JavaScript with full file system access, a compromised plugin could exfiltrate vault contents to an external server. UNSAID: Obsidian's security model for community plugins provides minimal sandboxing: plugins run in the main renderer process with access to the Vault API (all files), requestUrl (arbitrary HTTP requests), and Node.js APIs. A malicious plugin could silently send vault contents to a remote server, modify notes, or delete files. The risk vectors: a popular plugin with a new malicious maintainer (supply chain attack), a counterfeit plugin with a name similar to a popular one, or a plugin with undisclosed tracking code. Mitigation: enable 'Restricted Mode' and only disable it for plugins you've reviewed or that come from maintainers with established community reputation. Before installing, check the plugin's GitHub: recent commits, star count, and open issues. The Obsidian team reviews plugins for the community store but is not exhaustive — relying solely on the store review as a security guarantee is insufficient for sensitive vaults.

Logseq's graph visualization includes a 'Pages' filter panel that lets users isolate subsets of the graph by namespace, tag, or property for focused exploration rather than viewing the overwhelming full graph. UNSAID: The Logseq graph view renders all pages as nodes and all links as edges by default, which is visually overwhelming for graphs above 200 pages. Use the Filters panel (eyeball icon) to isolate: Pages in a specific namespace (e.g., show only 'Projects/*' pages), Pages with a specific tag (#book, #person), Journal pages only, or Pages modified within a date range. The graph settings allow adjusting: link distance (spring strength), center force (how tightly clustered), repel force (node spacing), and charge. For making sense of knowledge clusters: enable 'orphan pages' filter to find isolated notes, then investigate why they lack connections. The local graph view (right-click any note > Open local graph) shows connections up to 5 hops away from the selected note, making it a useful exploration tool for active research sessions.

Obsidian vault for freelance/consulting practice management combines client notes, project tracking, meeting records, invoice tracking, and time logging in a unified knowledge base. UNSAID: Client note structure: one page per client with frontmatter (name, company, contact, status: active/prospect/archived, hourly_rate). Project notes link to client notes with frontmatter (client, start_date, end_date, status, budget). Meeting notes are dated and link to both project and client notes. Time logging: append daily time entries to a 'Time Log' note with format: '- [[Client Name]] [[Project]] 2.5h #billable description.' Dataview query aggregates billable hours per client per month for invoice generation. The invoice note uses a Templater template that pulls client rate from the client note's frontmatter and hours from the time log query to calculate total invoice amount. The entire practice becomes queryable: which clients generated the most revenue this year, which projects ran over budget, which client types have the best hourly effective rate.

evidence_refs.description: Dataview for business tracking queries in Obsidian

statement:
Designing a Logseq workflow for team meeting notes with shared action item tracking requires careful convention design since Logseq doesn't have native multi-user features. UNSAID: For small teams (2-5 people) sharing a Logseq graph via Logseq Sync or git: create meeting pages named '[[YYYY-MM-DD Team Meeting]]' with properties: attendees:: [[Name1]], [[Name2]], facilitator::, decisions::. Action items use TODO blocks with assigned:: property: 'TODO Review the proposal
assigned:: [[John]]'. A shared 'Action Items' query page shows all open TODOs across all meeting pages, filterable by assignee. The critical workflow discipline: every meeting ends with explicit action item creation in Logseq rather than email follow-up, keeping the context in the graph. Conflict management: team members should not edit the same day's meeting notes simultaneously. The team coordinator 'closes' a meeting note by adding a 'status:: reviewed' property after distribution, preventing late edits that confuse the shared history.

The long-term value of PKM tools like Obsidian and Logseq is realized only through consistent daily use over years — the compounding effect of accumulated notes and connections becomes the primary source of value, not the tool's features themselves. UNSAID: Research on note-taking tools shows that users who take structured notes daily for 2+ years report qualitatively different thinking experiences: ideas from years-old notes surface unexpectedly as relevant to current problems, creative connections appear between domains that weren't consciously linked, and the act of writing notes becomes a thinking clarification process rather than just storage. This compounding effect is why PKM practitioners describe their note system as a 'second brain' — not metaphorically, but as a genuine extension of working memory across time. The key behavioral requirement: write notes WHEN you're reading or thinking, not later from memory. Deferred capture loses the context and nuance that makes notes valuable. The enemy of PKM is perfectionism — better to have an imperfect note captured now than a perfect note planned but never written.

evidence_refs.description: Building a Second Brain by Tiago Forte

statement: Obsidian Hover Editor plugin (community) allows opening linked notes in floating popup windows without leaving the current note, enabling reference checking without context switching. UNSAID: The default Obsidian 'hover preview' shows a read-only popup when hovering over links. Hover Editor upgrades this to a fully editable panel with pinning capability — pin a hover editor open to keep reference material visible while writing in the main note. Multiple hover editors can be pinned simultaneously, creating a multi-panel workflow without rearranging the workspace layout. For research writing, pin: the outline of the document you're writing, a reference literature note, and a permanent note you're developing. The critical difference from split panes: hover editors float over the current view without changing the layout, and they close when dismissed (less visual clutter than permanent split panes). Hover Editor also extends the hover preview API that other plugins use, so installing it upgrades hover previews vault-wide.

Setting up Obsidian for GTD (Getting Things Done) methodology requires mapping GTD constructs (Inbox, Next Actions, Projects, Waiting For, Someday/Maybe, Reference) to Obsidian's file and tag system. UNSAID: GTD in Obsidian: Inbox folder receives all uncaptured thoughts via QuickAdd (hotkey → 'Inbox/YYYY-MM-DD-HH-MM.md'). Daily review processes inbox to: Next Actions list (a single note with checked-off items), Projects folder (one note per project), Waiting For section in relevant project notes, Someday/Maybe folder, or Reference folders. The critical GTD requirement: weekly review that processes all inbox items, reviews all project notes, checks Waiting For list. Templater generates the weekly review checklist. Dataview shows: all items tagged #next-action, all projects with no recent modification (stalled), and all Waiting For items by delegate. The GTD purist objection to Obsidian: GTD emphasizes trusted system + trusted capture device, but Obsidian's capture on mobile (compared to inbox email or a notes widget) adds friction. Supplement with a simple mobile capture that feeds into Obsidian during desktop review.

Obsidian footnotes and LaTeX math rendering can create invisible rendering failures when the same vault is used across desktop (full rendering) and mobile (limited plugin support) without awareness. UNSAID: Obsidian desktop renders LaTeX math via MathJax/KaTeX natively — '$E=mc^2$' and '$$\sum$$' render as formatted equations. Obsidian mobile also renders basic LaTeX, but some advanced packages and complex multi-line equations fail silently (show as raw LaTeX text). Footnotes '[^1]' render correctly on both platforms. The failure mode: an academic researcher creates complex LaTeX diagrams (using tikz package) in Obsidian desktop, which render beautifully there but appear as raw code on mobile or when shared. The fix: use only the MathJax/KaTeX common subset (not full LaTeX) for cross-platform compatibility, or use Excalidraw for diagrams instead of tikz. For equation-heavy academic writing, Obsidian's LaTeX support is sufficient for standard equations but insufficient for diagrams that require specialized LaTeX packages.

Logseq's 'right sidebar' provides a multi-pane workspace for simultaneously viewing multiple pages and query results without switching away from the current page. UNSAID: Open any page or query in the right sidebar by Shift+clicking a link instead of clicking it normally. The sidebar can hold multiple panels stacked vertically, each collapsible. For a research session: right sidebar contains the 3-4 source pages you're synthesizing from, while the main editor contains the synthesis you're writing. Clicking links in sidebar panels also opens them in the sidebar (Shift+click opens new panel, regular click replaces current sidebar panel). The sidebar persists across sessions until manually closed. For daily journaling with reference: pin your project's TODO query in the sidebar so outstanding tasks are always visible while capturing today's journal entries. The sidebar also hosts the flashcard review panel, enabling parallel note-taking while reviewing cards.

Creating an Obsidian vault for solopreneur business intelligence combines market research, competitor tracking, customer interview notes, and product decisions in a searchable knowledge graph. UNSAID: Business intelligence vault structure: /Research/Competitors/ (one note per competitor with frontmatter: name, funding, pricing, key_differentiator, last_updated), /Research/Market-Trends/ (trend tracking notes with date), /Customers/ (interview notes with frontmatter: name, company, pain_points, use_case), /Decisions/ (ADR-style notes for major product/business decisions). Dataview-powered dashboards: Competitor Intelligence Dashboard (shows all competitors sorted by last_updated, surfacing stale research), Customer Insights Dashboard (groups customer notes by pain_point tag, revealing patterns), Decision Log (chronological list of all major decisions with rationale). The vault becomes organizational memory — when onboarding a new team member, the decision log explains why the product is the way it is. Link customer quotes directly to feature notes to maintain customer-problem-solution traceability.

evidence_refs.description: Dataview for business intelligence in Obsidian

statement: Obsidian and Logseq vault sizes are bounded by file system performance, not application limits — vaults above 50,000 files start experiencing OS-level file enumeration delays that manifest as slow vault loading and search indexing. UNSAID: Obsidian loads on startup by enumerating all files in the vault directory (recursively). On macOS, file enumeration of 50,000 files takes 5-10 seconds; on Windows (NTFS, slower than APFS), 10-20 seconds. Search index building is proportional to total file size. Mitigation: keep attachments (PDFs, images) in a separate directory outside the vault and link to them using absolute paths, or use a dedicated media storage service (iCloud, Dropbox) for large files. Note content files are typically small (5-50KB each) — 10,000 notes is only 500MB, well within performance. The actual bottleneck is file count, not total size. For vaults accumulating daily screenshots: configure Obsidian to compress images on paste, or use an external image storage service (Imgur, Cloudinary) and link to hosted images instead of embedding local files.

Obsidian's 'Linked mentions' and 'Unlinked mentions' panels in the right sidebar reveal both explicit wikilinks and coincidental text occurrences, enabling retroactive link creation for notes that mention topics without linking to them. UNSAID: The Backlinks panel (right sidebar or core plugin view) shows two sections: Linked Mentions (notes that explicitly [[link]] to the current note) and Unlinked Mentions (notes that contain the current note's name as plain text without linking). Unlinked mentions are opportunities to create explicit links — hover over a mention and click the 'Link' button to convert the text to a wikilink. For a large vault, periodically reviewing Unlinked Mentions for key concept notes (MOCs, important permanent notes) surfaces connections you didn't consciously create when writing. The 'Match case' toggle in backlinks is important — 'Python' as a proper noun shows different results than 'python' lowercase. The Dataview 'file.inlinks' property only counts Linked Mentions, not Unlinked — so true semantic connectivity requires explicit linking.

Obsidian's command palette (Ctrl+P) and Vim mode together represent the keyboard-first philosophy that makes Obsidian significantly faster for touch-typists compared to GUI-heavy tools like Notion. UNSAID: The command palette surfaces every available action (500+ with plugins) via fuzzy search — users who learn to drive Obsidian via command palette rather than menus become dramatically faster. 'Close current tab,' 'Move file to folder,' 'Open Templater,' 'QuickAdd: Add to Inbox' — all accessible without mouse movement. Vim mode (Settings > Editor > Vim key bindings) adds modal editing that reduces hand movement from keyboard to mouse. The combination: touch-typists who invest 2-4 weeks learning Obsidian's command vocabulary report that note-taking friction disappears entirely, and the tool becomes an extension of thinking rather than an interruption. This keyboard-first capability is why developers and writers disproportionately adopt Obsidian — it rewards the investment in learning the tool with compounding efficiency gains.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian hotkeys and command palette documentation

statement: Building a research-to-publication pipeline using Obsidian, Pandoc, and LaTeX produces professional-quality academic documents from markdown source while maintaining the benefits of plain-text note management. UNSAID: Pipeline: write paper sections in Obsidian as individual markdown files (abstract.md, introduction.md, methodology.md, etc.), combine via a 'main.md' file that uses Pandoc include directives or Obsidian Longform. Export command: 'pandoc main.md -o paper.pdf --template=ieee --bibliography=refs.bib --citeproc'. The Zotero Better BibTeX plugin auto-exports refs.bib whenever the Zotero library changes, keeping the bibliography current without manual export. Pandoc templates for common academic formats (IEEE, APA, Chicago) are available from the Pandoc community. Citations in Obsidian use '@citekey' syntax that Pandoc converts to formatted citations with bibliography. The advantage over LaTeX-first workflows: Obsidian's backlinks and Dataview queries provide project management during writing, while Pandoc handles final typesetting. For journal submission requiring Word format, Pandoc converts the same markdown to DOCX with the bibliography formatted correctly.

evidence_refs.description: Pandoc documentation for academic document conversion

statement: The complete Obsidian/Logseq ecosystem stack for a knowledge worker in 2025 combines: Obsidian (writing and reference), Logseq (capture and thinking), Readwise (reading pipeline), Zotero (research citations), Anki (spaced repetition), and Excalidraw (visual thinking), each connected through their respective integrations. UNSAID: The integration map: Readwise → Obsidian (via Readwise plugin, auto-importing highlights from Kindle/Twitter/articles/podcasts), Zotero → Obsidian (via Zotero Integration plugin, literature notes), Obsidian → Anki (via Obsidian-to-Anki plugin, flashcard export), Excalidraw → Obsidian (native .excalidraw file format), Logseq ↔ Obsidian (shared markdown folder). The weekly rhythm: Logseq daily journaling + capture → Readwise review and note promotion → Obsidian permanent note development → Anki card review → occasional Obsidian to Anki sync. This stack handles the full knowledge lifecycle: input (Readwise), storage (Zotero), thinking (Logseq), writing (Obsidian), visual thinking (Excalidraw), and retention (Anki). Total monthly cost: ~$15 (Readwise Reader), others free/one-time. The key to making this work: ruthless curation — only the most important material makes it through each stage.

Obsidian Live Preview mode represents the optimal editing experience for most users, rendering markdown formatting in-place while keeping it editable, unlike Reading mode (non-editable) or Source mode (raw markdown). UNSAID: Live Preview (enabled by default since Obsidian 0.13) renders bold, italic, code, and headers without switching modes — clicking any rendered element reveals the underlying markdown for editing. Source mode is essential for diagnosing rendering issues and developing plugins/snippets. Reading mode disables editing and renders embeds and Dataview queries — use Reading mode for reviewing final documents and for presentations. The key Live Preview limitation: some complex markdown constructs (multi-line HTML, complex table syntax) render incorrectly in Live Preview but correctly in Reading mode — when in doubt, check Reading mode. Plugin developers should test in both Live Preview and Source mode since DOM structures differ. The 'Focus mode' (via Minimal theme or dedicated plugin) hides UI chrome in Live Preview for distraction-free writing.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian editing and preview modes documentation

statement: Logseq Zotero integration links academic papers to Logseq graph pages, enabling block-level citations that connect arguments in notes to specific paragraphs in source papers. UNSAID: Configure via Settings > Zotero > Set Zotero API key. The /zotero slash command in Logseq searches Zotero library and creates a linked literature page. Block-level citing: after importing a Zotero item, use the generated page's citation key (e.g., [[smith2024learning]]) to reference the paper from any block. Unlike Obsidian's Zotero Integration (which creates full literature notes), Logseq's integration creates lightweight pages with metadata that grow organically as you add annotation blocks. For annotation import, Logseq can import Zotero notes/highlights attached to items, creating a block structure matching the PDF structure. The practical limitation: Logseq's Zotero integration is less feature-rich than Obsidian's — no template customization, limited metadata import. For heavy academic workflows, Obsidian + Zotero Integration is more powerful. Logseq suits researchers who want quick citations in daily notes without elaborate literature note structure.

Obsidian Sync's 'Excluded folders' feature silently stops syncing excluded folders even when those folders contain critical plugins or configuration, creating desync between devices. UNSAID: Obsidian Sync's excluded folders configuration (Settings > Sync > Excluded folders) prevents specified folders from syncing. Users commonly exclude '.obsidian/plugins/' to save sync storage, not realizing this means each device must independently install and update plugins. If one device has a critical plugin (e.g., Dataview with specific version) and another device has a different version, query results and note rendering can differ between devices. Worse: plugin data stored in '.obsidian/plugins/<plugin-name>/data.json' (plugin-specific settings) also doesn't sync for excluded plugins. The recommended pattern: sync '.obsidian/' entirely except for large cached files ('.obsidian/plugins/*/main.js' files are large but can be re-downloaded). Use Selective Sync thoughtfully — only exclude clearly non-essential folders like '.obsidian/workspace.json' which stores window layout (device-specific).

Building a startup founder's knowledge base in Obsidian with investor relations, hiring pipeline, product roadmap, and competitive landscape creates an unfair information advantage for decision-making speed. UNSAID: Founder vault structure: /Investors/ (one note per investor: frontmatter with name, fund, stage, check_size, portfolio_companies, last_contact, status), /Team/ (notes per employee: join date, equity, skills, 1:1 notes), /Competitors/ (competitor profiles), /Roadmap/ (feature notes linked to customer requests), /Decisions/ (dated decision records), /Metrics/ (monthly metrics notes). The power of this structure: before a board meeting, a Dataview query generates the investor update with all relevant metrics from monthly notes. Before fundraising, the investor pipeline dashboard shows relationship strength and timing. The competitive landscape note aggregates signals from multiple sources (funding news linked from dated research notes, product changelog observations) creating a live competitive intelligence feed. The founder's most critical practice: weekly update to the 'Metrics' monthly note to maintain the dashboard's accuracy.

Converting paper notes (handwritten notebooks, printed PDFs) into searchable Obsidian content uses OCR tools (Apple Intelligence, Microsoft Lens, or Google Keep) combined with structured digitization workflow. UNSAID: For handwritten notes, the mobile OCR workflow: photograph with Apple Notes (built-in OCR makes text selectable) or Microsoft Lens (better OCR for printed text), copy text, create Obsidian note from the OCR output. For print-heavy PDFs, use pdftotext (macOS CLI) to extract text: 'pdftotext file.pdf - | pbcopy' copies all PDF text to clipboard for pasting into Obsidian. The structuring step is critical: raw OCR output is unstructured — run it through a Templater processing script that calls an LLM API (via Text Generator plugin) to structure the content into a standard note format. Quality check: always review OCR output for errors, especially technical terms and proper nouns. For ongoing handwriting conversion, an iPad with Apple Pencil + Goodnotes/Notability provides simultaneous handwriting + OCR with export to Obsidian via the companion app share sheet.

Logseq's open-source DB version (announced 2024) fundamentally changes the tool's trust model: data is no longer plain-text markdown but an encrypted SQLite database, creating portability trade-offs that the community is actively debating. UNSAID: The DB Logseq transition from file-based markdown to SQLite means: individual pages are no longer editable outside Logseq, git diffs of notes become binary rather than readable, and third-party tools that read the Logseq folder can no longer access content. The migration from file-based to DB format is one-way (with migration tool); the reverse is lossy. The Logseq team argues the trade-offs are worth it: CRDT-based sync, millisecond-latency queries, block versioning, and multi-graph references are all enabled by the DB model. For users who prioritize portability and plain-text ownership (the original Logseq selling point), the DB version is a regression. The community response has been mixed: enthusiasts excited about sync improvements, and core users concerned about the departure from the markdown-first philosophy. The file-based Logseq version will continue to be maintained during the transition period.

evidence_refs.description: Logseq DB version announcement blog post

statement: Obsidian Workspace plugin allows saving and restoring entire window layouts (open tabs, panel positions, sidebar state) as named workspaces, enabling context switching between different work modes. UNSAID: Save a workspace via Command palette > Manage workspaces > Save. Common workspace configurations: 'Writing mode' (single focused editor, no sidebar), 'Research mode' (split panes, backlinks visible, graph in sidebar), 'Daily review mode' (daily note + backlinks + task query). Switch workspaces in seconds rather than manually rearranging panels. The limitation: workspaces save layouts but not the content of opened notes — after restoring a workspace, each pane opens its last active note. For reproducible research sessions, create a dedicated project workspace that opens the project MOC, the active task list, and the current working note. Workspaces are stored in '.obsidian/workspaces.json' and can be version-controlled for sharing workspace configurations with teammates who use the same vault.

Logseq's 'Re-index' and 'Clear cache' operations should NOT be confused — re-index rebuilds the Datascript DB from files (slow, 5-10 min for large graphs), while clear-cache removes the render cache (fast, fixes display glitches). UNSAID: The 'Clear cache' operation in Logseq is equivalent to a browser cache clear — it removes cached rendered views but not the underlying data. Most display problems (block not appearing after edit, tag count wrong in sidebar) are fixed by clear-cache and a restart. Re-index rebuilds the entire database from scratch by re-reading all markdown files — use only when queries return incorrect results that clear-cache doesn't fix, or when switching between multiple graph instances. The hidden risk: re-index on a large graph during an active editing session can overwrite unsaved changes if Logseq reads file content before the current edit buffer is flushed to disk. Always save all open pages before running re-index. Logseq's auto-save interval (configurable, default 500ms) means most users never notice unsaved changes, but explicit save (Ctrl+S) before re-index is a safe practice.

evidence_refs.description: Logseq settings and cache management documentation

statement: Obsidian for creative writing (fiction, worldbuilding) uses a combination of Longform plugin for manuscript management, Character/Location notes for lore tracking, and Dataview for consistency checking across a complex fictional world. UNSAID: Worldbuilding vault structure: /Characters/ (one note per character with frontmatter: name, age, faction, first_appearance, traits), /Locations/ (geographic and setting notes), /Timeline/ (dated event notes with year:: and event_type:: properties), /Lore/ (laws, magic systems, historical context), /Manuscript/ (Longform project folder). Consistency checking via Dataview: which characters appeared in scenes before their established introduction chapter (timeline inconsistency query), which locations are referenced but have no lore note (missing worldbuilding), and which character traits established in Act 1 aren't mentioned again in Act 3 (forgotten character details). The graph view becomes a story structure diagram when characters and locations are properly linked in scene notes — clusters reveal which characters share the most scenes. For collaborative fiction writing, Obsidian-git with a shared private repo provides version-controlled manuscript collaboration without paid sync services.

evidence_refs.description: Longform plugin for fiction manuscript management

statement: The ideal PKM workflow isn't about finding the perfect tool — it's about developing consistent capture and processing habits that work with any tool, because tool-switching disrupts the compounded value of accumulated notes more than any feature improvement justifies. UNSAID: Studies of PKM practitioners show that tool-switchers report higher initial excitement but lower long-term output than consistent users of the same tool for 3+ years. Each tool switch involves: migration overhead (hours to weeks), habit relearning, link rebuilding (wikilinks in Obsidian don't transfer as live links to Logseq's block references), and a 'honeymoon phase' where exploring the new tool substitutes for actual knowledge work. The decision to switch tools should be driven by a concrete, persistent workflow failure that the current tool cannot address, not by feature envy or new tool syndrome. The PKM meta-lesson: every hour spent optimizing the system is an hour not spent generating output WITH the system. A 'good enough' system used consistently beats a 'perfect' system always being improved.

evidence_refs.description: Evidence for consistent note practice over tool optimization

statement: Obsidian's 'Random Note' feature combined with a review checklist template turns serendipitous note discovery into a systematic knowledge renewal practice. UNSAID: The Random Note command (core plugin) opens an unpredicted note from the vault. When this becomes a daily 5-minute habit: open 5 random notes, for each ask 'what connections does this have to current projects?' and either add links, update the note with new thinking, or schedule it for deeper review. This practice combats the 'library syndrome' where notes are captured but never revisited. Research on spaced learning shows that retrieval practice (recalling information) strengthens memory more than re-reading — randomly encountering old notes and consciously connecting them to current thinking achieves a similar retrieval effect without formal scheduling. For PKM practitioners with 1000+ notes, the probability of rediscovering a relevant note through normal navigation drops to nearly zero — random note review is the only systematic way to mine the accumulated vault.

Logseq integrated with Omnivore (open-source read-later service) creates a fully open-source reading pipeline where articles, newsletters, and web pages flow into Logseq as structured pages with full-text search. UNSAID: Omnivore is a free, open-source alternative to Readwise Reader — it has browser extension, mobile app, email forwarding (for newsletters), and Logseq/Obsidian sync. The Logseq Omnivore plugin (community) syncs saved articles and highlights to Logseq pages, with configurable templates. Unlike Readwise's subscription model, Omnivore is self-hostable (Docker) for privacy-conscious users. The Omnivore → Logseq sync creates one page per article with properties: url, author, labels, read date, and nested blocks for highlights. This enables Logseq queries like: '{{query (and (property type
) (property label

))}}' to surface unreviewed research articles. The integration supports scheduled sync (e.g., morning daily note auto-imports overnight reading) making the reading capture→vault pipeline nearly automatic.

Obsidian's table editing experience in Source mode and Live Preview mode is significantly worse than dedicated tools, creating friction for note types with large tables. UNSAID: Markdown table syntax requires careful column alignment and pipe character management — even in Live Preview mode, inserting or deleting rows/columns is cumbersome compared to spreadsheet tools. The Advanced Tables plugin (community) dramatically improves the experience with tab-to-next-cell navigation, formula support, and automatic column alignment. However, Obsidian tables are still not competitive with Notion's database tables or Airtable for data-heavy workflows. Tables with 10+ columns or 50+ rows are difficult to navigate in Obsidian — consider using CSV attachments (embedded as ![[file.csv]]) for large datasets, or linking to an external spreadsheet. The Dataview table view is often better than native Markdown tables for data that lives in frontmatter, since it renders dynamically and doesn't require manual table formatting.

Logseq's 'Contents' page serves as a table of contents for the entire graph — curated links to major topic areas, project hubs, and frequently-accessed pages that provides a starting point superior to the left sidebar. UNSAID: The 'Contents' page in Logseq is a special page referenced as the Favorite page — it's the default landing page when opening a graph. Structure it like a personal homepage: links to active projects, links to key concept areas, a query showing today's journal preview, and a query showing unresolved TODOs. Unlike static folder structures, the Contents page can include live queries alongside manual curation. Update the Contents page whenever starting a new project (add the project link) or completing one (move to an Archive section). For teams sharing a Logseq graph, the Contents page serves as the organization's knowledge home page — new team members start here to orient themselves. The Contents page is also the ideal place for cross-graph links (referencing pages from other Logseq graphs) to create a multi-graph navigation hub.

Migrating a personal wiki from MediaWiki to Obsidian involves converting wiki markup to markdown, restructuring inter-page links, and establishing a new organizational system that leverages Obsidian's graph-based model. UNSAID: Export MediaWiki via Special:Export as XML, then use the pandoc command 'pandoc -f mediawiki -t markdown file.xml -o output.md' for individual page conversion or a batch conversion script. MediaWiki templates ({{template}}) don't convert — they become placeholder text. Categories in MediaWiki map to Obsidian tags (replace '[[Category:Tech]]' with '#Tech'). Internal MediaWiki links '[[Page Title]]' convert directly to Obsidian wikilinks, a rare format compatibility. The restructuring challenge: MediaWiki's hierarchical category tree (pages belong to categories, categories have parent categories) has no direct Obsidian equivalent — replace the category hierarchy with MOC notes and tags. For large wikis (1000+ pages), the conversion is a multi-week project; prioritize migrating actively-used content first, leaving archive content for later.

evidence_refs.description: Pandoc MediaWiki to Markdown conversion documentation

statement: Obsidian's 'Minimal' theme and the matching Minimal Theme Settings plugin represent the most widely adopted theme in the Obsidian ecosystem because it prioritizes readability and performance over visual complexity. UNSAID: Minimal theme (by kepano) has consistently been the most downloaded Obsidian theme for 3+ years. Its design philosophy: reduce visual noise (thinner headings, less prominent backlink styling, cleaner sidebars) while improving content readability (optimized line height, measure for reading, careful font choices). The Minimal Theme Settings plugin provides a GUI for configuration without CSS knowledge. The key design insight: the default Obsidian theme prioritizes feature visibility over content readability — Minimal inverts this to center the note content. The 'Focus' mode in Minimal hides all non-content UI, turning Obsidian into a distraction-free writing environment that outperforms dedicated writing apps. For users who spend more time reading their notes than configuring them, Minimal's performance advantage (lighter CSS, fewer repaints) becomes noticeable in large vaults.

Logseq's scheduled backup (automatic to a local folder) is NOT a disaster recovery backup — it backs up to the same machine that could fail, providing only protection against accidental deletion within Logseq. UNSAID: Logseq's Settings > Backup creates periodic copies of the graph folder to a local backup location. This protects against: 1) Accidentally deleting a page within Logseq, 2) Graph database corruption that damages the current files. It does NOT protect against: drive failure, theft, fire, ransomware (which encrypts both original and backup on the same machine). For real disaster recovery: combine Logseq local backup with git remote (push to GitHub private repo) and/or Logseq Sync (cloud backup). The 30-day local backup history is sufficient for 'I accidentally deleted something' recovery but the offsite copy is the critical protection layer. Many users discover this distinction only after a hardware failure wipes both the original graph and the local backup simultaneously.

Obsidian hotkey configuration is a high-leverage investment — 2 hours spent assigning and memorizing 10 custom hotkeys reduces daily friction enough to compound into hours saved per month. UNSAID: Default Obsidian hotkeys cover basic navigation but leave common plugin commands (QuickAdd, Templater, Dataview refresh) without hotkeys. Assign hotkeys to the 10 actions you perform most frequently: new note from template (Ctrl+Shift+N), QuickAdd inbox capture (Ctrl+Shift+I), open daily note (Ctrl+D), navigate back (Alt+Left), switch workspace (no default — assign one), focus mode toggle, graph view, local graph, random note, and search in current file. The mnemonics matter: assign based on first letter when possible (D=Daily, I=Inbox). Print the custom hotkey list and tape it near your monitor for the first week — the muscle memory typically forms within 5-10 days of consistent use. For users adopting Vim mode, the normal mode commands ('gf' = follow link, 'g]' = next heading) eliminate even more mouse usage.

Logseq for software engineering teams as a technical wiki combines architecture decision records, runbooks, onboarding guides, and incident postmortems in a queryable, linked knowledge graph. UNSAID: Engineering wiki structure: /ADR/ (one page per architecture decision, properties: status, deciders, date, context, decision, consequences), /Runbooks/ (operational procedures with step-by-step blocks), /Onboarding/ (role-specific guides), /Incidents/ (postmortem pages with severity, timeline, root_cause, action_items properties). Logseq's block reference system enables sharing runbook steps across multiple runbooks without duplication — a common authentication step block is referenced in multiple service runbooks. Queries surface: all open ADRs needing decision, all incidents without resolved action items, and all runbooks not updated in 180 days (stale operational knowledge). The Logseq team sharing setup requires careful coordination since the graph is flat-file with git — establish branch-per-major-doc conventions to reduce merge conflicts. GitHub integration (CI checks on merged PRs) can validate that all linked pages exist and required properties are filled.

The Obsidian and Logseq ecosystem represents a counter-movement to algorithmic information consumption — these tools require intentional, reader-controlled knowledge curation rather than passive consumption of feed-delivered content. UNSAID: The PKM movement emerged partly as a reaction to the attention economy's infinite scroll, algorithmic feeds, and metrics-driven engagement that reward consumption over synthesis. Obsidian/Logseq enforce the opposite: nothing enters the vault automatically (except through deliberate integrations like Readwise), every connection is user-created (not algorithm-suggested), and the 'feed' is replaced by personal curation and review rituals. This is why PKM practitioners report a qualitative change in their relationship with information — from passive consumer to active curator. The deeper implication: time spent in Obsidian/Logseq is competitive time against social media, newsletters, and feed readers. The PKM lifestyle requires defending deliberate note-processing time against the lower-friction alternative of passive consumption. Users who successfully maintain PKM practices typically develop explicit 'information diet' rules that govern what enters the vault.

evidence_refs.description: PKM as counter to algorithmic consumption

statement: Obsidian's Outline core plugin and the Better Outline community plugin provide document navigation for long notes via a hierarchical heading table of contents in the right sidebar. UNSAID: The Outline plugin (enabled by default) shows all headings in the current note as a clickable tree in the right sidebar panel. For notes longer than 2000 words, clicking heading links in the outline is faster than scrolling. Better Outline (community) adds: collapsible heading sections in the editor, drag-to-reorder headings, and fold/unfold all levels at once. The critical writing practice: use consistent heading hierarchy (# for title, ## for major sections, ### for sub-sections) to get maximum value from the outline. Notes without headings (pure prose or bullet lists) don't benefit from the Outline plugin. For long-form writing, the outline panel becomes the writing plan made visible — you can see the entire document structure while writing any section. The Obsidian Zoom plugin (community) allows 'zooming into' a single heading's content, hiding everything else for focused section editing.

Setting up Logseq for a doctor or medical professional requires configuring for HIPAA-compliant note storage, de-identification practices, and clinical knowledge organization around specialties and patient types. UNSAID: For compliant medical note-taking: never include real patient identifiers in Logseq — use case identifiers ([[Case-2024-031]]) that reference a separate HIPAA-compliant system. The Logseq graph stores medical knowledge (clinical patterns, drug interactions, procedure notes) NOT patient records. Structure: /Clinical-Knowledge/ (condition profiles, differential diagnosis frameworks), /Procedures/ (step-by-step procedural checklists), /Evidence/ (literature summaries with evidence grade), /Cases/ (de-identified teaching cases). The block reference system is ideal for clinical protocols where sub-steps are shared across multiple procedures. Logseq's offline-first architecture makes it suitable for clinical environments with limited internet access. For medical students, Logseq + Anki flashcards creates a comprehensive learning system: clinical knowledge accumulates in Logseq, high-yield facts export to Anki for active recall. The query system surfaces patterns across specialties that support diagnostic reasoning.

evidence_refs.description: Logseq security considerations for medical use

statement: Obsidian's 'File recovery' core plugin snapshots note content every 5 minutes but these snapshots are stored in '.obsidian/backups/' — a folder that many users exclude from sync, making recovery impossible on other devices. UNSAID: File Recovery (core plugin) creates local snapshots that allow rolling back individual notes to earlier versions (up to configured retention period, default 7 days). These snapshots are in '.obsidian/backups/' which is device-local by default. If you delete a note on Device A, the backups folder on Device B doesn't help since backups are local. Obsidian Sync creates separate version history for synced vaults, which IS cross-device accessible. The two systems are complementary but easily confused: File Recovery = local-only undo history; Obsidian Sync version history = cloud-accessible recovery. For the safest setup: enable File Recovery with 30-day retention, use Obsidian Sync for cross-device recovery, and obsidian-git for off-site backup. The failure scenario: user deletes a note, realizes the mistake 2 weeks later — File Recovery may have it; Obsidian Sync definitely has it (if used); git has it if committed.

Logseq's 'Linked references' section at the bottom of every page aggregates all blocks across the graph that reference the current page, creating an emergent inbox for each topic page. UNSAID: The Linked References section is the most powerful feature of Logseq's bidirectional linking — it shows every block mentioning [[Page Name]] in context, grouped by the source page. This transforms topic pages into living summaries: a [[Python]] page accumulates every code snippet, tutorial note, and insight block that mentions Python without any manual organization effort. The pattern creates a second review surface: periodically scrolling through the Linked References of your most important pages reveals insights captured months ago that are now relevant to current work. For project pages, Linked References serves as a chronological project history — every meeting note, research capture, and decision block that referenced the project appears here in reverse-chronological order. This eliminates the need for explicit project notes in many cases — the aggregated references ARE the project record.

Obsidian's attachment embedding (images, PDFs, audio) increases vault size and sync times proportionally, requiring deliberate attachment management strategies for media-heavy workflows. UNSAID: Each embedded attachment is a binary file that: increases Obsidian Sync storage consumption (counted toward the subscription limit), increases git repository size (binary files don't compress well and can't be diffed), and slows vault loading (thumbnail generation). For image-heavy vaults: configure 'Maximum image width' in Settings to auto-resize pasted images to a max dimension (e.g., 1200px) — this reduces file size 10-30x for high-res phone photos without visible quality loss. Consider linking to external image hosting (Cloudinary, Imgur, or a self-hosted file server) instead of embedding. For PDFs, store in an external reference manager (Zotero, DEVONthink) and link rather than embed — PDFs are the largest files in most research vaults. The Attachment Management plugin (community) automatically moves attachments to a folder next to the note that uses them, preventing the vault root from becoming cluttered with orphaned attachments.

Creating an Obsidian knowledge management system for a legal professional combines case notes, statute references, jurisdiction tracking, and precedent databases while maintaining strict client confidentiality. UNSAID: Legal knowledge vault structure: /Practice-Areas/ (one MOC per area: contracts, IP, employment), /Statutes/ (key statute notes with citation, jurisdiction, effective_date frontmatter), /Case-Law/ (precedent notes linked to practice area MOCs), /Matters/ (per-matter notes using non-identifying codes, linked to practice area), /Templates/ (contract clauses, motion frameworks). Client confidentiality enforcement: matter notes use code names (Matter-2024-Corporate-001) without client identifiers; actual client information stays in the firm's case management system (Clio, MyCase). Dataview surfaces: statutes amended in the last 6 months (search for recent_amendment frontmatter), case law from the current jurisdiction for a practice area, and open matters with no recent activity. The practice area MOC becomes the attorney's externalized memory of the legal landscape, reducing time spent re-researching familiar areas.

Obsidian's 'Tabs' view (multiple tabs like a browser, introduced in Obsidian 1.0) combined with Tab Groups enables a project-context workspace where related notes stay open together across work sessions. UNSAID: Open multiple notes as tabs (Ctrl+Click link or right-click > Open in new tab). Tab groups split the workspace horizontally or vertically, each group with its own tab bar. Use case: left tab group contains project context (MOC, ongoing notes), right tab group contains the active writing surface. Tabs persist across restarts — reopening Obsidian returns to the last session's tabs. For long research projects, keeping the key reference notes pinned as tabs (right-click tab > Pin) prevents them from being replaced when navigating. The practical limit: 10+ open tabs becomes cognitively overwhelming — use workspaces (different workspace = different tab set) to maintain project-specific tab contexts that can be quickly switched. For note types you visit daily (daily note, inbox), pin them so they always appear at the left edge of the tab bar.

Logseq's 'pages' folder and 'journals' folder default locations can conflict with Obsidian vault structure when using both tools on the same folder, causing Obsidian to display Logseq's folder structure as unwanted clutter. UNSAID: Logseq defaults: pages in the root directory, journals in 'journals/' subfolder, assets in 'assets/' subfolder. Obsidian defaults: notes in root, attachments in 'attachments/' subfolder. The conflict points: 1) Logseq creates journal files with date format 'YYYY_MM_DD' (with underscores, not hyphens), while Obsidian daily notes default to 'YYYY-MM-DD'. 2) The Logseq 'assets' folder contains binary files that Obsidian shows in its file panel. 3) Logseq's 'pages' property metadata files ('logseq/metadata.edn') confuse Obsidian which treats them as notes. Resolution: in Logseq Settings, change the journal file name format to match Obsidian's daily note format, configure assets to use Obsidian's attachment folder, and add '.edn' extension to Obsidian's excluded files list. These configurations make the two tools cohabitate cleanly in the same directory.

Obsidian URI protocol ('obsidian://') enables deep linking from external applications (email, calendar, Notion) to specific Obsidian notes and commands, creating a bidirectional connection between Obsidian and the rest of the digital workflow. UNSAID: Obsidian URI format: 'obsidian://open?vault=VaultName&file=NoteName' (opens specific note), 'obsidian://new?name=NoteName&content=Content' (creates new note). These URIs work from: terminal commands, calendar event descriptions, email clients, Alfred/Raycast launchers, web browsers. Practical use cases: calendar events with Obsidian links to the meeting prep note, email signatures linking to your contact page, Raycast/Alfred shortcuts that open daily note or QuickAdd capture. The Copy Obsidian URL command (built-in) copies the current note's obsidian:// URL to clipboard. For automation (Shortcuts on iOS/macOS, Keyboard Maestro on macOS), Obsidian URIs enable triggering Obsidian note creation or opening from any external trigger. Warning: sharing obsidian:// links with non-Obsidian users results in broken links — they only work for people with the same vault installed.

Creating a Logseq productivity system aligned with the CODE framework (Capture, Organize, Distill, Express) from Building a Second Brain maps to Logseq's natural workflow primitives. UNSAID: CODE in Logseq: Capture = daily journal entries with quick block creation (no organization friction), Organize = page links automatically categorize content into topic pages (organization happens through linking, not filing), Distill = periodic review of Linked References to find the most important insights (create a permanent summary page from recurring themes), Express = create output pages that reference and synthesize the distilled insights (a blog post draft, a presentation outline, a decision memo). The key Logseq advantage for CODE: the Organize step is nearly automatic — every [[link]] you add during capture creates categorization without any additional effort. Compare to folder-based systems where capture and organization are separate steps, creating organizational debt. The friction point: Distill requires intentional review time that must be scheduled — it doesn't happen automatically. Weekly or monthly reviews specifically for distillation (scanning Linked References on active topic pages) prevent the system from stagnating.

evidence_refs.description: Building a Second Brain CODE framework by Tiago Forte

statement: Obsidian's 'Files and links' settings panel controls link format, attachment location, and note creation behavior — configuring these correctly at vault creation prevents costly reorganization later. UNSAID: Critical settings to configure at vault creation: 1) 'Default location for new notes': choose 'In the folder specified below' and set to '/Inbox/' to ensure new notes land in a processing queue rather than the vault root. 2) 'Attachment folder path': set to './{note-folder}/assets/' to keep attachments adjacent to the notes that use them (prevents orphaned attachments). 3) 'Use wikilinks': disable for cross-tool compatibility, enable for Obsidian-native features like auto-updating links on rename. 4) 'Detect all file extensions': enable to see all files in the vault including non-markdown (CSV, PDF). Changing 'New note location' after the vault is active requires manually moving all orphaned root-level notes — a 30-minute cleanup for a 200-note vault. The Inbox/Processed workflow (all new notes to Inbox, processed notes to final locations) prevents the vault root from becoming a disorganized dumping ground.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian files and links settings documentation

statement: Logseq's 'Journals' feature creates mandatory daily pages that serve as the primary capture surface, making it harder to 'lose' important thoughts compared to free-form note creation in other tools. UNSAID: Every time you open Logseq, today's journal page is created and opened automatically — you can start typing immediately. This default behavior enforces the daily capture habit without conscious effort. The journal entry order (newest at top of the Journals page) creates a reverse-chronological archive of your daily thinking. The block-level journal structure allows different sections within a day to link to different topic pages, creating a fine-grained intellectual timeline. For users transitioning from paper journals, Logseq's journal-first design matches the 'write in chronological log, retrieve by topic' workflow more naturally than folder-based tools. The psychological benefit: an empty daily page at startup is an invitation to capture, not a confrontation with organizational decisions. Compare to tools that require choosing where to save a note before writing it.

Using Obsidian as a team documentation wiki without establishing naming conventions and link hygiene guidelines results in the vault fracturing into disconnected islands of notes with broken links within months. UNSAID: Team vault failures typically follow this pattern: each team member captures notes with personal naming conventions, creating duplicate pages (John's 'Python Best Practices', Sarah's 'Python Tips', Mike's 'Python Notes'), broken links when someone renames their file, and topic pages that accumulate references only from the person who created them. The fix requires upfront governance: team-agreed naming conventions (enforced by Linter plugin), a team Contents page that's the authoritative starting point, a naming authority (designated team member who resolves naming conflicts), and a quarterly link audit (Dataview query for orphaned notes). Teams that skip governance and start with 'we'll figure it out as we go' consistently report needing a major vault reorganization within 6 months. For teams under 5 people, Notion's structured database model may be less powerful but requires less governance than Obsidian's free-form approach.

The block-reference model (Logseq) vs the page-link model (Obsidian) represents fundamentally different epistemologies about the atomic unit of knowledge — ideas vs documents. UNSAID: Logseq's block model treats the paragraph or bullet point as the minimum meaningful unit — each idea is atomic and can be linked or embedded independently. This matches how Niklas Luhmann's physical Zettelkasten worked: each card (block) was an atomic idea with a unique address. Obsidian's page model treats the note as the unit — a note can be atomic (one idea) or synthetic (a collection of related ideas), and the granularity is user-defined. The practical difference: in Logseq, you can create a reference from any block in page A to any block in page B, creating a fine-grained idea network. In Obsidian, you link pages to pages, with heading-level links as a coarser option. Neither model is universally superior — atomic block references are better for non-linear, network-style thinking (Zettelkasten, research synthesis), while page links are better for hierarchical, document-style knowledge (technical documentation, creative writing). Most users succeed with whichever model matches their primary knowledge use case.

evidence_refs.description: Zettelkasten atomic note concept and epistemology

statement: Archiving a completed Logseq project while maintaining searchable history requires a specific export-and-tag approach that preserves links without polluting the active graph. UNSAID: When a project completes: 1) Create an 'archived' property on the project page (archived:: true). 2) Tag all project-related pages with #archived. 3) Run a query to verify all project pages are tagged. 4) Add to Logseq's 'Hidden pages' list (Settings > Hidden pages) any pages that should no longer appear in the default page search. The pages remain in the graph and are still discoverable via search, but they don't appear in autocomplete suggestions. For very old projects, export the project's pages to a separate archive graph. The critical insight: deleting project pages removes the historical record and breaks any links from other pages — always archive instead of delete. The archive tagging approach allows a future query to surface 'lessons from archived projects' type analyses, which is valuable institutional memory. Configure Logseq's favorites sidebar to show only active project pages to maintain a clean working view.

Obsidian's 'Natural Language Dates' plugin enables writing date references in human language ('next Monday', 'in 3 weeks', '@today') that the plugin converts to ISO date links, improving the natural feel of date entry. UNSAID: The NLD plugin (community, by argenos) intercepts '@date_text' patterns as you type and converts them to [[YYYY-MM-DD]] links on confirmation. Supported patterns: @today, @tomorrow, @next-week, @in-2-weeks, @2024-03-15, @monday. Converted dates are wikilinks, so the Daily Notes for those dates automatically get linked references from any note mentioning them. For task scheduling in Obsidian (without Tasks plugin), the NLD pattern is efficient: 'Review the proposal @next-friday' creates a date link that Dataview can query. The NLD plugin also provides a date picker UI (command palette > NLD: Date Picker) for mouse-based date selection. The limitation: NLD only converts '@' prefixed natural language — plain text dates ('March 15') are not converted. Combine with Templater for date variables that don't need natural language input but benefit from the picker UI.

Logseq's multi-column layout (CSS hack via custom themes or the Multi-Column plugin) enables Kanban-style project boards within Logseq's outliner, combining the block-reference power with visual project management. UNSAID: Logseq's default outliner is single-column. The Multi-Column plugin (HACS/community) adds a directive syntax that renders multiple sibling blocks side-by-side. Use case: a project page with 'To Do', 'In Progress', 'Done' sections arranged as columns — drag blocks between columns to update status. Unlike dedicated Kanban apps, these cards are Logseq blocks that can reference other pages, contain nested checklists, and appear in queries. The implementation requires careful block structure: the column parent block must have the multi-column class, and Logseq's CSS must support the plugin's styles. For simpler kanban needs, a query-based approach (separate query blocks filtered by status property) achieves similar visualization without the multi-column complexity. Custom CSS-only multi-column layouts are available in community themes for users who prefer not to add plugins.

Obsidian's Publish service has a 4GB storage limit per site and no built-in search for published content, requiring either Pagefind integration or external search solutions for large published vaults. UNSAID: Obsidian Publish creates a hosted website from your vault's selected notes at obsidian.site/your-slug or a custom domain. The built-in navigation is folder and backlink-based — there is no search bar by default on published sites. Large published vaults (500+ notes) need search to be useful for visitors. Integration solution: Pagefind (open-source, static search library) can be added to Obsidian Publish via a custom CSS injection method, creating a client-side search index. Quartz (self-hosted alternative to Publish) includes Pagefind search natively. For Obsidian Publish specifically, the search limitation is a significant UX gap — new visitors can't find content by keyword, only through the vault structure. The 4GB limit affects media-heavy published vaults with many embedded images and PDFs. Obsidian Publish does not render Dataview queries for visitors, making dashboard-style notes appear as empty code blocks on the published site.

The success of Obsidian's community plugin ecosystem (1000+ plugins, millions of downloads) demonstrates that a composable, markdown-native tool with an open plugin API creates more value than a feature-complete but closed tool. UNSAID: Obsidian ships with a minimal core set of features and exposes a TypeScript API that enables community plugins to add arbitrarily complex functionality without degrading the base experience. This architecture means: power users get exactly the features they need (Dataview, Templater, Kanban), while casual users get a clean, uncluttered writing tool. Compare to Notion (closed, feature-is-a-product decision) and Roam Research (limited API, founder-controlled feature roadmap). The composability principle means Obsidian has no direct competitor with equivalent functionality — the combination of Dataview + Templater + obsidian-git + Excalidraw + QuickAdd creates a unique tool that couldn't be built by a single company with a feature roadmap. The risk: popular plugins can become critical infrastructure, and plugin abandonment creates maintenance crises for workflows built around them. The community responds by forking abandoned plugins, maintaining continuity.

evidence_refs.description: Obsidian plugin API and community ecosystem documentation

statement: Onboarding a new user to Obsidian or Logseq most effectively requires a 'start small, add complexity' approach — beginning with just core note-taking before introducing plugins, templates, and automation. UNSAID: The common onboarding failure: a PKM enthusiast recommends an elaborate system with 12 plugins, PARA structure, Templater scripts, and Dataview dashboards to a new user. The new user spends 2 days configuring the system and zero time taking notes, then abandons the tool. The research-supported alternative: Week 1 — write daily notes, create links when naturally remembering other content. Week 2 — add first plugin (Dataview or Tasks if needed). Week 3 — create first template for the most repeated note type. Month 2 — evaluate what friction points remain and add targeted solutions. This progressive onboarding matches how skilled PKM practitioners actually developed their systems — iteratively, over months, guided by real friction rather than anticipated needs. The best PKM setup is the simplest one that removes the specific friction the user actually experiences, not the theoretically most powerful system.

Obsidian's 'Note Composer' plugin (core, 2022+) enables splitting and merging notes, making vault reorganization as easy as selecting text and extracting it to a new note with automatic link creation. UNSAID: Two primary operations: 1) Extract selection to new note — select any text block, run 'Extract current selection', Obsidian creates a new note from the selected text and replaces it with a wikilink to the new note. 2) Merge current file into another — moves the current note's content into a target note, with optional link replacement.
